  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Fork+It%2C+Try+It%2C+Roll+It+Back"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing Replit's forkable, reversible infrastructure"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are exotic inventions on their own. Copy-on-write storage is a well-known technique. Git is decades old. Multi-agent systems and parallel sampling are active, widely-discussed ideas across the whole AI coding space. What makes Replit's engineering story genuinely compelling is how deliberately these pieces were connected: infrastructure originally built to make human developers' lives easier fast copying, easy versioning turned out to be exactly the foundation needed to let an AI agent work boldly and autonomously, while still promising that any mistake it makes can always, cheaply, be undone.

## What This Means Going Forward

As AI agents take on more of the actual building not just suggesting code, but planning, executing, and fixing entire applications with a database attached the interesting engineering questions increasingly aren't about the model's intelligence at all. They're about exactly what this article has covered: how do you let something powerful and somewhat unpredictable act freely, without ever risking something you genuinely can't get back. Replit's answer, running through nearly every piece of this article, is the same one echoed elsewhere in this series: don't try to make the AI perfectly cautious. Make the environment around it so cheaply reversible that caution becomes less necessary in the first place.


---

## How Supabase Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-supabase-works
Published: 2026-07-18T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Supabase's architecture, how a schema becomes an instant API, how database changes stream live to your screen over a write-ahead log, and how a connection pooler was built to handle a million connections to Postgres at once.


You create a table in Supabase's dashboard, and before you've written a single line of backend code, you already have a working REST API for it. Insert a row from your frontend, and a moment later, every other open tab watching that table updates live, no polling, no refresh. It feels like a lot of separate products stitched together. It's actually one very deliberate bet: instead of inventing a new kind of database, build everything around the one nearly every backend engineer already trusts PostgreSQL.

This article walks through how that bet is actually engineered turning a database schema into an API automatically, streaming live changes out of Postgres's own internal log, and solving the very real problem of a serverless world sending far more database connections than Postgres was ever designed to handle. As with the rest of this series, this draws on public documentation, Supabase's own engineering blog, and independent technical breakdowns, not their literal internal source code.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=One+Schema%2C+Many+Services%2C+All+Postgres"
  alt="Illustration of a Postgres database powering multiple connected services"
/>

## The Core Bet: Don't Reinvent the Database, Unbundle It

A lot of "instant backend" tools ask you to learn their own proprietary way of storing and querying data which is fine, until you outgrow it, and suddenly migrating away feels like moving to a different country and learning a new language on your way out the door.

A deep dive on [Startupik](https://startupik.com/supabase-postgres-deep-dive-architecture-and-scaling/) makes the point plainly: Supabase leans hard into standard PostgreSQL features tables, views, roles, triggers, replication, functions, JSONB, extensions, plain SQL which is exactly why teams keep their relational integrity, real joins, real transactions, and mature tooling they already know, rather than trading it away for something exotic. The same piece is direct about why this matters: your database schema, indexes, functions, and row-level policies aren't just backend implementation detail here they effectively become the application's real engine.

Think of it like buying a car built entirely from parts you can get serviced at any regular mechanic, instead of one built with proprietary parts only the manufacturer can touch. You get less "magic," but you're never trapped.

## From a Table to a Live API, Automatically

Here's the part that feels like a party trick the first time you see it. You create a table called `todos` with a few columns, and without writing a controller, a route, or an ORM model you can already `GET`, `POST`, `PATCH`, and `DELETE` rows through a real REST API.

The same [Startupik breakdown](https://startupik.com/supabase-postgres-deep-dive-architecture-and-scaling/) explains this is powered by PostgREST, a tool that auto-generates RESTful endpoints directly from your database schema meaning a table becomes a production API without anyone hand-writing controllers. Under the hood, your client SDK talks to this REST layer, or to Auth, or to Realtime, or to a database function but the same source is clear that most of those roads eventually lead to the same place: Postgres itself.

```text
You create a table: `todos (id, title, is_done, user_id)`
  → PostgREST inspects the schema
  → Generates: GET /todos, POST /todos, PATCH /todos?id=eq.1, DELETE /todos?id=eq.1
  → Every request still goes through Postgres's own permission system
  → No hand-written backend route required
```

Imagine a filing cabinet where the labels on the drawers automatically become the instructions for how to request, add, or remove documents from them you never had to separately write a memo explaining "here's how you ask for a file from drawer 3." The structure itself generates the interface.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Schema+In%2C+REST+API+Out"
  alt="Diagram of PostgREST generating an API directly from a database schema"
/>

## Row Level Security: The Real Authorization Layer

Important detail that trips up a lot of people new to Supabase, because it flips the usual mental model. In a lot of backend frameworks, "who's allowed to see this data" is a rule written in your application code an `if` statement in a controller somewhere. In Supabase, the database itself is expected to enforce that rule.

A production playbook from [FrontendTechLead](https://www.frontendtechlead.com/blog/supabase-production-architecture-2026) describes Row Level Security (RLS) as effectively becoming your real authorization layer a set of policies attached directly to your Postgres tables, checked by the database itself on every single query, regardless of whether that query came through the auto-generated REST API, a Realtime subscription, or a raw SQL client.

It's the difference between a bouncer standing at the door of just one entrance to a building, versus every single door inside the building, including the supply closets, having its own lock that checks your ID before it opens no matter which entrance you originally walked in through. Because RLS lives in the database itself, it can't be accidentally bypassed just because a request came in through an unexpected path.

## Realtime: Listening to a Database Think Out Loud

This is genuinely one of the most technically interesting parts of the whole platform, and it leans on a feature of Postgres that most developers never touch directly.

Supabase's own documentation on [Realtime Architecture](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/realtime/architecture) explains the foundation: every Postgres database keeps a Write-Ahead Log, or WAL an internal, ordered record of every change made to the data, which Postgres itself uses for its own durability and replication. Supabase's Realtime service creates a logical replication slot on your database and streams that WAL, parsing out the individual insert, update, and delete events, and forwarding each one out to subscribed clients as a JSON message over a WebSocket connection.

A deeper breakdown on [Medium](https://medium.com/@ansh91627/building-scalable-real-time-systems-a-deep-dive-into-supabase-realtime-architecture-and-eccb01852f2b) explains why this specific approach logical replication rather than something bolted on top of the application layer matters: it captures changes at the database level itself, which means literally any way a row gets modified (your API, a background job, someone running raw SQL) still gets picked up and broadcast, because Realtime isn't watching your application code, it's watching the database's own internal record of what happened.

```text
Someone updates a row in Postgres
  → Change is written to the Write-Ahead Log (WAL), as Postgres always does anyway
  → Supabase Realtime is tailing that WAL via a logical replication slot
  → The change is parsed into a JSON event
  → Routed to the Erlang process representing the right client's socket
  → Delivered over WebSocket, live, to every subscribed screen
```

It's like being able to listen in on a librarian's own internal notebook where they jot down every single book they shelve or remove, rather than having to ask the librarian directly and hope they remember to tell you. Because you're reading their private, ongoing record of the truth, you never miss an update, no matter which door someone used to interact with the library.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Tailing+the+Write-Ahead+Log"
  alt="Diagram of Realtime streaming changes from Postgres's write-ahead log"
/>

### Why Elixir, of All Languages

Here's a technology choice that seems unusual at first glance, until you look at what job it's actually doing. The same Supabase documentation explains that Realtime is built on Phoenix, a framework written in Elixir chosen specifically because Elixir provides extremely lightweight, virtual-machine-level processes rather than heavy operating-system-level ones, which makes it very good at handling an enormous number of simultaneous, mostly-idle WebSocket connections at once.

Imagine a call center where instead of hiring one dedicated employee per phone line expensive, and mostly sitting idle waiting for a call you have a system that can juggle hundreds of thousands of lightweight "attention slots," each one only actually doing work the instant its specific line rings. That's roughly the advantage Elixir's lightweight process model gives Supabase's Realtime service when it's holding open connections for potentially millions of mostly-quiet clients.

The same documentation adds a nice resilience detail too: every Realtime region runs at least two nodes, specifically so that if one node goes offline, the other can reconnect and pick up streaming changes without the whole region going dark.

## Broadcast and Presence: When You Don't Need the Database at All

Not every "live" feature actually needs to touch your database. A production guide from [FrontendTechLead](https://www.frontendtechlead.com/blog/supabase-production-architecture-2026) draws a clear distinction: "Postgres Changes" (listening for inserts, updates, and deletes) is the easiest option, but every single change gets filtered per connected client and has its permissions re-checked individually meaning thousands of subscribers watching one busy table is a well-known way to hit a real scaling wall.

For things like a shared cursor moving around a collaborative document, someone's "typing…" indicator, or a live list of who's currently online, the same guide recommends Broadcast sending ephemeral messages directly between clients (or from the server) without ever touching the database at all and Presence, a feature built on top of Broadcast specifically for tracking who's currently active in a channel, with automatic join and leave detection.

Checking a database on every keystroke to show someone's live cursor position is like mailing a letter every time you want to say "I'm still in the room" technically it works, but it's a wildly expensive way to communicate something this lightweight and temporary. Broadcast skips the mailbox (the database) entirely for exactly this kind of message.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Postgres+Changes+vs+Broadcast+vs+Presence"
  alt="Diagram distinguishing database-backed realtime from ephemeral broadcast messaging"
/>

## The Problem Nobody Sees Coming: Too Many Connections

This is a genuinely fascinating engineering story, and a great example of an old, well-behaved piece of technology running into a brand-new usage pattern it was never designed for.

Postgres, as a database, was designed decades ago around the assumption of a relatively small number of long-lived connections think a handful of application servers, each holding a steady connection open for a long time. Modern serverless applications flip that assumption on its head: potentially thousands of short-lived functions, each spinning up, briefly needing a database connection, and disappearing again seconds later.

Supabase's own engineering blog, in a post called [Supavisor: Scaling Postgres to 1 Million Connections](https://supabase.com/blog/supavisor-1-million), explains the standard fix most teams reach for first: PgBouncer, a connection pooler that sits in front of Postgres and shares a small number of real database connections across a much larger number of incoming client requests, running on the same instance as the database to keep latency low. But the same post notes a real trade-off with that setup: it competes for the same compute resources as the database it's protecting.

The team's answer was building Supavisor, described in the same post as architecturally different: only a single node holds the actual direct connection to a given database instance, and when you add more databases or read replicas, Supavisor spreads connections across them, with every instance in the cluster able to either execute a query directly (if it holds the connection) or relay the request to whichever node does. In their own reported load test, the same blog post describes successfully sustaining 20,000 queries per second 1.2 million queries per minute from a simulated one million concurrent client connections.

```text
The old way: PgBouncer
  App → PgBouncer (same box as Postgres) → Postgres
  Simple, low latency, but competes with Postgres for the same resources

The new way: Supavisor
  App → Any Supavisor node (cluster-aware)
    → If this node holds the DB connection: execute directly
    → If not: relay to the node that does
  Scales connection handling independently from the database itself
```

PgBouncer is like a single receptionist standing right at the door of the one office that does all the actual work, funneling visitors through efficiently, but still sharing the same hallway and lighting bill as that office. Supavisor is more like a whole network of receptionists across several buildings, each able to either help you directly or point you to exactly the right building so a sudden crowd of visitors doesn't crowd out the actual workers trying to get things done.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Supavisor%3A+Pooling+Connections+at+Cluster+Scale"
  alt="Diagram of Supavisor distributing database connections across a cluster"
/>

## Everything Auto-Scales, Because the Alternative Is a 3am Page

A guide on [Gaincafe](https://gaincafe.com/blog/built-to-scale-supabase-grows-with-your-startup-mvp-to-ipo) paints a genuinely relatable, slightly stressful scenario: a social app gets featured somewhere, traffic spikes 50x overnight, and suddenly the database crashes, auth stops working, and file storage maxes out, all at once, while the support inbox fills up with angry messages. The same piece describes Supabase's answer to this as automatic scaling built into each layer independently connection pooling absorbing bursts of concurrent requests, read replicas automatically distributing read-heavy traffic across multiple instances, and compute resources scaling with actual demand rather than a fixed, manually-provisioned ceiling.

The underlying idea connects directly back to everything covered above: connection pooling exists specifically so a sudden burst of serverless functions doesn't overwhelm Postgres directly, and read replicas exist so that heavy reporting queries or public read traffic don't compete with the writes your actual users are waiting on.

## Edge Functions and Storage: Rounding Out the Platform

It's worth briefly noting the pieces beyond the database itself. Supabase bundles Edge Functions serverless functions running on Deno, deployed close to users, for the kind of custom backend logic that doesn't fit neatly into a database policy or a generated REST endpoint alongside Storage, an S3-compatible file storage layer for the actual files (images, videos, uploads) a table full of URLs would otherwise just be pointing at.

The pattern holds even here: rather than inventing a brand-new file storage protocol, Storage deliberately stays S3-compatible, echoing the same philosophy running through the entire platform build compatibility with what people already trust, rather than asking them to trust something new just because it's yours.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Postgres+at+the+Center%2C+Everything+Else+Orbiting+It"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing Supabase's Postgres-centric architecture"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are exotic on their own. Auto-generating a REST API from a schema is a known idea. Streaming a database's write-ahead log is a documented Postgres feature, not a secret. Connection pooling is decades old. What makes Supabase's architecture genuinely interesting is the consistency of the underlying bet, repeated at every layer: don't replace Postgres, don't replace S3, don't invent a new query language take what backend engineers already trust, and build the smallest possible amount of new infrastructure needed to make it feel instant, live, and automatically scalable.

## What This Means Going Forward

As more products lean on Supabase for genuinely demanding, real-time, high-connection-count workloads collaborative tools, live dashboards, AI applications juggling both vector search and realtime updates the same tensions covered here only get more important to understand well: knowing when a live feature actually needs the database (and Row Level Security's protection) versus when it's just an ephemeral broadcast message, and knowing that a serverless world means connection pooling isn't an optional nicety, it's load-bearing infrastructure. The lesson underneath Supabase's story isn't really about Postgres, or Elixir, or Deno specifically. It's the same one running through this whole series: the fastest way to build something that feels brand new is often to get remarkably good at orchestrating things that are already old, trusted, and thoroughly battle-tested.


---

## How Dropbox Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-dropbox-works
Published: 2026-07-17T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Dropbox's architecture, the custom-built Magic Pocket storage system holding exabytes of data, and Nucleus, the from-scratch Rust rewrite of the sync engine running on half a billion devices.


You drag a file into your Dropbox folder, and somewhere between one second and the next, it's already climbing onto a server, getting sliced up, encrypted, and quietly replicated across data centers you'll never see. Edit a single paragraph in a 4GB video's subtitle file, and Dropbox doesn't re-upload the whole thing it figures out exactly what changed and sends only that. None of this is visible. That's rather the point.

This article looks at two of the biggest engineering stories behind Dropbox: Magic Pocket, the custom storage system they built after outgrowing the cloud, and Nucleus, the ground-up rewrite of the sync engine that runs quietly on every device with the Dropbox app installed. Nearly everything here is drawn directly from Dropbox's own engineering blog, so we get to hear most of this straight from the people who built it.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Drag%2C+Drop%2C+Sync%2C+Repeat"
  alt="Illustration of a file being dragged into a synced folder"
/>

## Why Dropbox Left the Cloud It Grew Up On

You build your first version on borrowed infrastructure because it's fast to get started, and it works great, right up until the moment your own scale becomes the very problem you now have to solve.

Dropbox's own engineering blog, in its [introductory Magic Pocket post](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/magic-pocket-infrastructure), explains that Dropbox always ran a hybrid setup hosting file metadata and web servers in data centers they managed themselves, while storing actual file content on Amazon S3. The same post is generous about it too, crediting AWS as an invaluable partner that let them scale rapidly in the early years. But by the time the company had grown large enough, that arrangement stopped making sense, and building a dedicated, custom storage system eventually named Magic Pocket, after Dropbox's own early nickname for keeping all your files in one convenient place became a major initiative starting in the summer of 2013.

Here's the number that makes the scale of this real: a piece on [Built In](https://builtin.com/hardware/dropbox-magic-pocket-distributed-storage-system) explains that one exabyte is roughly the equivalent of a billion gigabytes, or about 245 million DVDs' worth of data and Dropbox's own blog on [scaling to exabytes](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/magic-pocket-infrastructure) describes moving over 90% of all user data onto this custom infrastructure within about two and a half years of starting the project.

## Blocks, Not Files: How Magic Pocket Actually Stores Your Data

Dropbox doesn't store your files as single, whole objects sitting on a disk somewhere. Dropbox's own architectural overview of [Magic Pocket](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/inside-the-magic-pocket) explains that files get split into blocks opaque chunks up to 4MB in size which are compressed and encrypted, and each one is given a name derived from a SHA-256 hash of its own content.

Think of it like a library that doesn't shelve entire books as single objects, but instead breaks every book down into individual pages, and files each page away by a unique fingerprint of what's written on it. If two different books happen to share an identical page somewhere, the library only needs to store that page once. That's exactly the advantage this block-based approach unlocks: identical chunks of data, wherever they show up across the entire platform, only need to be stored a single time.

```text
You upload a file
  → File is split into blocks (up to 4MB each)
  → Each block is compressed and encrypted
  → Each block is named by a SHA-256 hash of its content
  → Identical blocks across any file, any user, are stored only once
  → Blocks are grouped into 1GB "buckets" for storage and replication
```

A detailed breakdown on [Medium](https://medium.com/@anant7210/dropbox-magic-pocket-5ec95cc40a42) fills in the next layer: those blocks get grouped into 1GB buckets, sometimes called volumes, which serve as the actual unit of storage and replication. Roughly 1 petabyte lives per physical storage device, about 8 petabytes per rack, and around 50 petabytes per "cell" Magic Pocket's term for an independent storage unit.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Files+%E2%86%92+Blocks+%E2%86%92+Buckets+%E2%86%92+Cells"
  alt="Diagram of Magic Pocket's block-based storage hierarchy"
/>

## The Book Nobody Reads Cover to Cover Twice

This block-based design has a second, quieter benefit that matters just as much as deduplication: efficient sync. A write-up on [Akshay Ghalme's blog](https://akshayghalme.com/blogs/how-dropbox-delta-sync-works/) explains the practical payoff plainly editing a 4GB video only requires uploading the few hundred kilobytes that actually changed, not the whole file again, because the content-addressable, block-level structure means only the blocks that are genuinely new need to move over the network at all.

You wouldn't reprint and re-ship the entire book to everyone who has a copy you'd just send them the one corrected page, since every other page is identical to what they already have. That's essentially what Dropbox is doing every time you save a small change to a huge file.

## Append-Only, Immutable, and Built for the Hardware It Runs On

Here's a detail that shows just how far Dropbox's engineering went to match their software design to the physical realities of hard drives. Dropbox's own blog on their [SMR drive deployment](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/extending-magic-pocket-innovation-with-the-first-petabyte-scale-smr-drive-deployment) explains that Magic Pocket's blocks are organized into 1GB extents, written in an append-only fashion and treated as immutable once written meaning data is never edited in place, only added.

That design choice turned out to be a perfect match for a newer, denser kind of hard drive called SMR (Shingled Magnetic Recording), which are notoriously slower for random writes but excellent at sequential ones. Because Magic Pocket was already built around writing data sequentially and never modifying it afterward, adopting these cheaper, denser drives didn't require fighting the storage system's own architecture it fit naturally, which the same post credits with meaningfully reducing storage costs.

It's the difference between a diary where you only ever add new entries at the end, versus a document you're constantly going back to edit in the middle. The diary format is much easier to store efficiently on certain kinds of "pages" and Dropbox chose that format for its storage system on purpose, then picked hardware that rewarded the choice.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Append-Only%2C+Immutable%2C+Built+for+SMR"
  alt="Diagram of append-only storage matched to sequential-write hard drives"
/>

## What Happens When a Drive Just Dies

At the scale Dropbox operates, hardware failure isn't a rare event you plan around it's a daily, ongoing fact of life. A presentation described on [QCon San Francisco](https://qconsf.com/presentation/oct2022/magic-pocket-dropboxs-exabyte-scale-blob-storage-system) notes that Magic Pocket automatically identifies and repairs hundreds of hardware failures every single day, while still maintaining 99.99% availability.

The same Medium breakdown explains the mechanics behind this: block metadata is tracked in MySQL, indexed by block hash, recording exactly which volumes and storage devices hold a given block. If a device stays down past a certain threshold, a master process uses the block's other surviving replicas to re-copy it onto healthy devices elsewhere, tagging the new copy with a generation number specifically so that if the master itself crashes mid-repair, any stale, incomplete copies can be safely recognized and discarded rather than accidentally trusted.

Think of a library with three copies of every important book, spread across three different buildings. If one building has a fire, the library doesn't panic it just prints a new copy from one of the two surviving originals and ships it to a fourth building, quietly, while the readers who wanted that book never even notice the disruption happened.

```text
Device goes down
  → Master detects the failure past a threshold
  → Uses a healthy replica to re-copy the missing block
  → New copy gets a generation number
  → If master crashes mid-repair, generation numbers prevent
    a half-finished copy from being mistaken as valid
  → Replication factor is restored, silently, in the background
```

## Not All Data Deserves the Same Treatment

Not every file you've ever uploaded is equally important to have instantly available. A [TechCrunch report](https://techcrunch.com/?p=1822558) on Dropbox's cold storage layer explains the reasoning plainly most people upload files to Dropbox mainly for backup purposes, and then rarely touch them again after the first day or so.

Rather than treating a decade-old backup file exactly the same as a document you're actively editing right now, Dropbox's own blog on [optimizing for cold storage](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/how-we-optimized-magic-pocket-for-cold-storage) describes building a genuinely separate storage tier specifically for rarely accessed data running on the same disks and network as the rest of Magic Pocket, but organized in a way that significantly reduces cost without giving up durability or availability, and notably, without adding meaningful new architectural complexity elsewhere.

It's the difference between keeping your daily-use kitchenware in an easy-to-reach cabinet, versus storing the good holiday china in a box in the attic. Both are still safe, both are still yours whenever you need them but only one needs to be instantly within arm's reach every single day.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Warm+Storage+vs+Cold+Storage"
  alt="Diagram distinguishing frequently accessed data from rarely accessed archival data"
/>

## The Sync Engine: A 4-Year Rewrite They Almost Didn't Attempt

This next part of the story is a genuinely great lesson in engineering courage and restraint. The sync engine is the piece of software quietly running on your laptop, watching your Dropbox folder, deciding what needs to go up to the cloud and what needs to come down. Dropbox's own blog, in a post titled [Rewriting the Heart of Our Sync Engine](https://dropbox.tech/infrastructure/rewriting-the-heart-of-our-sync-engine), describes it as one of the oldest and most important pieces of code at the company and admits directly that rewriting it from scratch, a project that took roughly four years and was codenamed Nucleus, would have been a terrible idea in a lot of other environments.

The problem with the original engine, according to the same post, wasn't a lack of features it was a foundation that had accumulated years of subtle, hard-to-reproduce concurrency bugs, the kind where something "theoretically possible but extremely unlikely" would occasionally show up in production, and take hours to track down. A related InfoQ write-up on [testing the new sync engine](https://www.infoq.com/news/2020/04/dropbox-testing-sync-engine) explains the core issue even more concretely: the old client-server protocol allowed the client to reach genuinely invalid, orphaned states for example, receiving metadata about a file at a path before it had even received the parent folder that file was supposed to live inside.

Imagine trying to renovate a house one room at a time, except the foundation itself is slowly cracking in unpredictable places. At some point, patching individual rooms stops being the real fix you have to consider rebuilding the foundation itself, even though that's a much scarier, much bigger undertaking.

## "Design Away Invalid States": The Principle That Guided Everything

Here's the single idea that shaped the entire rewrite, and it's a genuinely useful lesson for any engineer building a complex system. The same InfoQ piece quotes Dropbox engineer Isaac Goldberg's explanation of Nucleus's core architectural principle: design away invalid system states, rather than trying to detect and handle them after the fact.

In practice, according to the same source, this meant representing the sync engine's state using three distinct trees: the remote filesystem state, the local filesystem state, and a "Synced Tree" recording the last known state both sides fully agreed on. That Synced Tree, the article explains, works conceptually like a merge base in version control it's what lets the system definitively answer the question "did the user change this file locally, or did it change on dropbox.com?" without ambiguity.

Dropbox's own blog on the rewrite adds that they redesigned the client-server protocol specifically for strong consistency guaranteeing the server and client agree on the same view of the remote filesystem before any change is even considered for syncing, closing off the exact category of "impossible but happens anyway" bug that plagued the original engine.

```text
Nucleus tracks three trees:
  Remote Tree    what the server currently has
  Local Tree     what's currently on your disk
  Synced Tree    the last state both sides agreed on (the "merge base")

Comparing all three tells the engine, unambiguously:
  → Did I change this file locally?
  → Did it change remotely?
  → Which direction does this sync need to go?
```

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Three+Trees%3A+Remote%2C+Local%2C+Synced"
  alt="Diagram of the three-tree state model behind Nucleus"
/>

## Why Rust, Specifically

This is a genuinely interesting technology choice, and Dropbox's own engineers are candid about exactly why they made it. The same rewrite blog post explains that Rust became a force multiplier for the team specifically because its ownership model let engineers reason safely about concurrent changes to shared state precisely the category of bug that had haunted the previous engine for years. An external analysis on [Convex's engineering blog](https://news.convex.dev/a-tale-of-three-codebases/) written by engineers who worked on Nucleus before founding Convex backs this up with real numbers: Nucleus now runs on over half a billion devices, manages trillions of files, and has to contend with every conceivable bizarre end-user filesystem configuration, all while maintaining what the same piece describes as an unblemished reliability track record.

Here's a relatable comparison for why "the compiler catches it before it ships" matters so much here: imagine an editor who doesn't just proofread your book after it's printed, but actually refuses to let certain sentence structures exist in the manuscript at all, because those structures are the exact pattern that's caused embarrassing errors in every previous book. Rust's ownership rules work a bit like that for concurrency bugs entire categories of mistakes simply can't compile in the first place, rather than needing to be caught later through painstaking, after-the-fact debugging.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Half+a+Billion+Devices%2C+One+Rewritten+Foundation"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing Dropbox's storage and sync architecture"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are exotic inventions on their own. Splitting files into content-addressed chunks is a known idea. Replicating data across machines for durability is standard distributed-systems practice. Rewriting a critical system in a memory-safe language is something plenty of companies have tried. What makes Dropbox's engineering story compelling is the scale and the discipline behind each decision: building an entire custom storage system rather than accepting the limits of someone else's cloud, and being brave enough to rebuild the most important, oldest, and scariest piece of client software from scratch but only after spending real time making sure the new foundation couldn't repeat the old one's mistakes by design, not just by good intentions.

## What This Means Going Forward

As Dropbox keeps expanding beyond simple file storage into collaboration and knowledge-work tools, the same foundational questions keep resurfacing in new forms: how do you keep exabytes of data both cheap and instantly durable, and how do you keep an ever-growing number of devices, file types, and collaboration patterns from ever colliding into the exact kind of "impossible but happens anyway" bug that forced a four-year rewrite the first time. The lesson underneath Dropbox's story isn't really about Rust, or SMR drives, or block hashing specifically. It's the same one running through this entire series: the software that feels the most invisible a folder that just quietly stays in sync is usually sitting on top of some of the most deliberately engineered decisions in the whole stack.


---

## How Postman Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-postman-works
Published: 2026-07-16T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Postman's architecture from the simple client-server design behind every request you send, to the microservices, Kubernetes migration, and Context Graph powering its newest AI agents.


Before Postman existed, testing an API meant writing a curl command by hand, or building a throwaway script just to check if an endpoint actually worked. It was clunky, easy to get wrong, and nobody enjoyed it. Postman turned that chore into a few clicks: pick a method, paste a URL, hit send, read the response. Something that simple, done well, ended up becoming the place millions of developers now design, test, document, and increasingly, let AI agents interact with APIs.

This article looks at how Postman is actually built from the everyday request you send as a solo developer, to the microservices and infrastructure running behind the scenes at company scale, to the newer AI layer being built on top of all of it. Most of the detail here comes straight from Postman's own engineering blog, so a lot of this is directly from the people building it.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Send%2C+Test%2C+Document%2C+Repeat"
  alt="Illustration of an API request being sent, tested, and documented"
/>

## It Started With One Developer's Annoyance

According to an interview with Postman's co-founder and CEO, Abhinav Asthana, on the [Stack Overflow blog](https://stackoverflow.blog/2025/12/04/postman-s-journey-and-unlocking-the-power-of-apis/), Postman began life as a side project built to solve his own personal frustrations with API development, started with just a couple of people sharing code between each other. Asthana himself, in the same interview, credits an unglamorous but important decision for a lot of what came after: keeping the architecture and product as simple as possible, deliberately resisting the urge to bolt on every exciting new framework or technology just because it existed.

That's a genuinely useful lesson buried in a scaling story the same interview notes Postman is now used by millions of developers, but the discipline that got it there wasn't chasing novelty, it was restraint.

## The Simple Version: What Happens When You Hit "Send"

Here's the part every Postman user has done a thousand times without thinking about the machinery behind it. A breakdown on [IGMGuru](https://www.igmguru.com/blog/what-is-postman) walks through the basic architecture: the Postman client acts as the API consumer, letting you define the request method, URL, headers, parameters, authentication, and body. Before that request actually leaves your machine, it passes through a request processing engine, which resolves any variables you've used, applies your chosen authorization, runs any pre-request scripts, and formats the final request making sure everything is valid before it's ever sent to the real API server.

Think of it like a strict but helpful assistant checking your travel documents before you get to the airport. It fills in your frequent flyer number, confirms your ID matches your ticket, double-checks nothing's missing so that by the time you actually reach the counter (the real API server), there's no back-and-forth, just a smooth handoff.

```text
You hit "Send"
  → Request Processing Engine resolves variables (e.g. {{base_url}})
  → Applies auth (API key, OAuth token, etc.)
  → Runs any pre-request scripts (e.g. generate a fresh timestamp)
  → Sends the fully formed request to the actual API server
  → Response comes back: status code, headers, body
  → Test scripts run automatically against that response
```

Once a response comes back, the same IGMGuru breakdown notes you can inspect every part of it individually status code, headers, cookies, and body and the scripting and test engine, running on JavaScript, automatically verifies things like status codes and response data against whatever checks you've written. String a bunch of these requests together with the Collection Runner, and you've got an entire workflow tested end-to-end, without touching each request by hand.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Request+In%2C+Validated%2C+Tested%2C+Response+Out"
  alt="Diagram of the request processing and testing pipeline"
/>

## Why Postman Cares So Much About Separating "Interface" From "Implementation"

This next idea is a little more abstract, but it's genuinely the philosophical core of why Postman exists as a company at all, not just a tool.

Postman's own blog, in a post announcing their [API Platform](https://blog.postman.com/announcing-new-postman-api-platform/), draws a clear line between two things people often lump together when they say "API": the **Interface** the language describing the API, usually expressed through protocols like HTTP using formats like JSON and the **Implementation** the actual code powering it, which might be written in Java, Node.js, or anything else. The same post makes the point directly: decoupling these two is what gives APIs their real power, because you can completely change how something works internally while keeping the interface developers rely on exactly the same.

Think of a restaurant's menu versus its kitchen. The menu (the interface) tells you exactly what you can order and roughly what to expect. The kitchen (the implementation) can completely change its recipes, swap suppliers, or hire a new chef, and as long as the dish on your plate still matches what the menu promised, you never notice or need to care. Postman's entire product exists to help teams design, test, and manage that menu reliably regardless of how often the kitchen changes behind it.

## Postman Building Postman: Microservices in Practice

Postman doesn't just sell tools for managing APIs, it actually runs on a whole landscape of its own internal APIs, built by its own engineering team, using roughly the same principles they preach to customers.

A post on [Postman's engineering blog](https://blog.postman.com/postman-engineering-microservices-example/) describes the company's internal structure as 12 cross-functional squads producing 40 different services, with each squad taking end-to-end ownership of their own roadmap, sprints, and tooling choices while still including dedicated members from design, product, security, and quality, not just engineers. The same post is refreshingly honest that this isn't some perfectly solved system: dependencies between squads still require real coordination, and teams are constantly re-evaluating trade-offs like whether a growing domain should stay a single microservice, split into more, or even fold back toward something more like a monorepo.

There's a nice bit of "eating your own dog food" here too the same blog post notes that Postman's own engineers are heavy users of Postman itself, acting as an early warning system: if something about the product is clunky, their own engineering team feels that pain directly, often before any customer does.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=12+Squads%2C+40+Services%2C+One+Platform"
  alt="Diagram of Postman's internal squad and microservice structure"
/>

## Taming the Infrastructure Behind the Scenes

A detailed post on [Postman's engineering blog](https://blog.postman.com/engineering/scaling-with-confidence-postmans-journey-to-infrastructure-as-code-with-kubernetes-and-argocd/) describes how their deployment pipeline used to be a mix of manual scripts and loosely enforced conventions workable at a small scale, but increasingly fragile as the platform grew. The same post is candid about the symptoms: environment drift was common, what worked fine in staging wouldn't always work the same way in production, rollbacks were painful because there was little confidence in what the last "known good" state even was, and onboarding a new engineer took real time simply because so much operational knowledge lived in people's heads rather than anywhere written down.

The fix, according to the same post, was migrating workloads onto Kubernetes for consistency across services and regions, standardizing deployments with Helm charts, and layering a custom internal tool called Parcels on top of ArgoCD specifically so engineers could describe the environment they wanted in plain terms "an environment that looks like production, except for these three services" instead of hand-editing raw YAML configuration files every time.

Imagine a growing restaurant chain where every location used to be built by a different contractor, with a different plan, remembered only by the one contractor who built it. Standardizing all of that into one blueprint, used consistently everywhere, is basically what Postman did with Helm and Kubernetes same idea, applied to server infrastructure instead of buildings.

```text
Old way:
  Manual scripts → tribal knowledge → environment drift → risky rollbacks

New way:
  Kubernetes (consistent deployment target)
    → Helm charts (standardized service definitions)
      → ArgoCD (declarative, automated syncing)
        → Parcels (self-service environments, no raw YAML needed)
```

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=From+Tribal+Knowledge+to+Infrastructure+as+Code"
  alt="Diagram of Postman's migration to Kubernetes and ArgoCD"
/>

## The Newest Layer: Teaching AI Agents to Understand APIs

This is where Postman's story gets genuinely interesting again, and it echoes a theme that's come up elsewhere in this series: the hard part of AI products often isn't the model, it's the structure and guardrails wrapped around it.

Postman's own blog, in a post explaining [how they really build production-grade AI agents](https://blog.postman.com/how-we-really-build-production-grade-ai-agents-beyond-models-toward-data-and-api-quality/), makes a pretty blunt argument: most AI agents that look great in a demo fail in production, and the core misunderstanding behind that failure is that teams over-invest in model intelligence and under-invest in the quality of the underlying system the data the model reasons over, the reliability of the tools it calls, and the governance wrapped around what it's allowed to do. The same post argues that if a new human engineer can't reliably use an API just from reading its specification, an AI agent calling that same API has no real chance either no amount of model quality fixes a badly documented, inconsistent API underneath.

That thinking shows up directly in Postman's newest feature. A post introducing the [AI Engineer](https://blog.postman.com/introducing-the-ai-engineer/) describes it as an agentic system built on top of something Postman calls the Context Graph described as an always-on, continuously updated map of the APIs and services across an entire organization. Point it at an API or application with a goal, and the same post explains it will explore endpoints, infer how things actually behave in practice (not just what the spec claims), and produce documentation, a working collection, and a real map of the system's actual behavior.

The same post breaks the design into three distinct layers, which is a genuinely useful mental model for how to build a trustworthy agent at all:

- **The Context Graph** defines which APIs exist, who owns them, and what's authorized, giving the agent the same guardrails a human engineer already operates within.
- **The Execution Layer** where the agent actually acts, inside a sandboxed cloud environment where it can pull repositories, run commands, and run tests, without ever touching production directly.
- **The Postman Toolset** the full set of Postman's existing capabilities, packaged specifically for the agent to call, rather than the agent improvising its own way of interacting with APIs.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Context+Graph+%2B+Sandbox+%2B+Governed+Tools"
  alt="Diagram of the AI Engineer's three-layer architecture"
/>

Think about why this three-layer split matters: imagine handing a new hire the keys to production on day one versus giving them a detailed map of the building, a supervised practice room to try things safely, and a clearly labeled toolbox of exactly what they're allowed to use. Both new hires might be equally capable but only one of them is set up not to accidentally break something important on their first day. Postman's own blog is explicit that this isn't a temporary compromise while AI catches up; it describes this human-in-the-loop, tool-bounded design as a stable architecture, because real-world systems are too messy and constantly changing for a fully autonomous agent to be reliable on its own.

## Why This All Connects Back to API Quality

Here's the thread tying the whole article together, and it's a genuinely insightful point buried in Postman's more recent writing. The same post on [building production-grade agents](https://blog.postman.com/how-we-really-build-production-grade-ai-agents-beyond-models-toward-data-and-api-quality/) argues that as agents increasingly become the ones actually calling APIs not just humans reading documentation APIs stop being simple integration points and start becoming something closer to the control plane for what AI systems are even capable of doing. If the data feeding a model is messy, or the API it's calling is inconsistent, those problems compound quickly across a multi-step chain of agent decisions.

That reframes everything discussed earlier in this article the request processing engine, the interface/implementation split, the internal microservices discipline, the move to reliable, standardized infrastructure not as separate, unrelated engineering stories, but as the same underlying discipline applied consistently over more than a decade: keep the interface clean, keep the underlying system honest, and trust gets built one reliable request at a time, whether the thing making that request is a person or, increasingly, an agent acting on their behalf.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Reliable+APIs%2C+Reliable+Agents"
  alt="Closing illustration connecting API quality to agent reliability"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of Postman's individual pieces are exotic inventions. A request-response client is an old idea. Microservices are a common pattern. Kubernetes and Helm are widely used elsewhere. What's interesting about Postman's story is how consistently the same instinct shows up at every layer, over more than a decade: keep the interface simple and trustworthy, and be deliberately careful about what you build underneath it whether "underneath it" means a JavaScript test engine, forty internal services, a Kubernetes cluster, or, most recently, an AI agent that needs the same guardrails a new human engineer would.

## What This Means Going Forward

As more software gets built and operated by AI agents rather than humans clicking through a UI, the interesting engineering question isn't really "how smart is the model." It's the same question Postman has quietly been answering since 2012: is the API underneath reliable, well-documented, and honest about how it actually behaves? Get that right, and both a junior engineer on their first day and an autonomous agent on its first task end up in the same good position able to trust the interface enough to actually get something done.


---

## How Slack Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-slack-works
Published: 2026-07-15T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Slack's architecture, the Channel Servers, Gateway Servers, and WebSocket connections that deliver messages across the globe in under 500 milliseconds, and the video-game origins that shaped it all.


You type a message in a channel with 10,000 members, hit enter, and somewhere between a few hundred milliseconds later, it shows up on 10,000 different screens some on laptops in Bangalore, some on phones in São Paulo, some on desktops that have been idle since lunch. Nobody refreshes anything. It just appears, like it was always going to.

That "it just appears" feeling is one of the harder problems in distributed systems, dressed up as something completely unremarkable. This article walks through how Slack actually pulls it off the persistent connections, the servers whose entire job is just deciding who should hear about a message, and a surprisingly fun origin story that shaped the whole thing. Most of the technical detail here comes straight from Slack's own engineering blog, so we're hearing a lot of this directly from the people who built it.

![Slack architecture diagram showing Channel Servers, Gateway Servers, and WebSocket connections](/slack.png)

## It Started as a Video Game

Here's a genuinely fun detail that turns out to explain a lot about why Slack is built the way it is. According to a breakdown on [ByteByteGo](https://blog.bytebytego.com/p/how-slack-supports-billions-of-daily), Slack's technical roots trace back to a browser-based MMORPG called Glitch. The game itself never took off commercially, but the internal communication tool the team built to coordinate while making it did and when Glitch shut down, that internal tool became the seed of what grew into Slack.

That inheritance shaped two lasting architectural habits, according to the same piece: a strong separation of concerns, where real-time messaging is handled by a dedicated system that's kept apart from the business logic much like how a game server keeps live player events separate from game rules and, as another write-up on [Talent500](https://talent500.com/blog/slack-architecture-real-time-messaging/) puts it, an early split between a dedicated channel server for real-time delivery and a separate web application for storage and authentication. It's a nice reminder that architecture decisions often outlive the product they were originally built for.

## The Four Core Servers

Slack's own engineering blog, in a post called [Real-time Messaging](https://slack.engineering/real-time-messaging/), describes the backbone of this system as four core Java services, each with one clear job:

- **Channel Servers (CS)** stateful, in-memory servers that hold the actual channel data and handle broadcasting messages to everyone subscribed.
- **Gateway Servers (GS)** the interface between a Slack client and the Channel Servers, holding user info and WebSocket subscriptions.
- **Admin Servers (AS)** stateless servers that sit between the web application backend and the Channel Servers.
- **Presence Servers (PS)** track who's currently online, powering the little green dot next to someone's name.

Imagine a busy restaurant. The kitchen (Channel Servers) actually prepares and plates every order. The waiters (Gateway Servers) are the ones actually standing at your table, taking your order and bringing you your food, without needing to know how the kitchen works internally. A back-office manager (Admin Servers) handles the paperwork side without ever touching a stove. And a separate greeter (Presence Servers) is just keeping track of who's currently in the building.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Channel%2C+Gateway%2C+Admin%2C+Presence"
  alt="Diagram of Slack's four core server types"
/>

## Getting Connected: What Happens When You Open Slack

You open the Slack app, and within a second, your unread messages, your channels, and your teammates' online status are all just... there. Slack's own blog walks through exactly how that happens.

When a client boots up, it first fetches a user token and WebSocket connection details from the Webapp backend described as a Hacklang codebase that also renders the actual Slack client interface. The client then initiates a WebSocket connection to whichever edge region is nearest to it, which gets routed by Envoy an open-source proxy Slack uses for load balancing and handling TLS to a Gateway Server. That Gateway Server then fetches the user's full channel list and other information from the Webapp, and sends the very first message down to the client to get it in sync.

```text
Client boots up
  → Fetches auth token + connection info from Webapp
  → Opens a WebSocket to the nearest edge region
  → Envoy routes the connection to a Gateway Server
  → Gateway Server pulls the user's channel list from Webapp
  → Gateway Server sends the client its initial state
  → Persistent WebSocket connection stays open for real-time updates
```

That persistent connection is the whole trick behind Slack feeling "live." Instead of your client repeatedly asking the server "anything new? anything new? anything new?" every few seconds, the server just pushes new information down the same open connection the instant it happens.

## Sending a Message: The Journey From Your Keyboard to Everyone's Screen

Here's where it gets genuinely interesting, and where an important design change tells a good lesson about trading a little bit of speed for a lot of safety.

A write-up on [Talent500](https://talent500.com/blog/slack-architecture-real-time-messaging/) explains that Slack's messaging path actually changed over time. Originally, messages went directly from the client to the channel server, which broadcast them and only persisted them to a database afterward fast, but risky, since a crash at just the wrong moment could lose a message the user had already seen marked as "sent." The fix was to change the order: the client now sends a message to the web app over a normal HTTP request, the web app logs and persists that message first, and only after it's safely stored does it hand off to the channel server for real-time broadcast.

It's the difference between shouting an announcement across a room before writing it down anywhere, versus writing it down in a notebook first, and only then reading it out loud. The second way is a little slower, but you can never lose the announcement, even if you get interrupted mid-sentence.

Slack's own blog describes what happens once that broadcast actually kicks off: a message sent in a channel travels through the Webapp API, to an Admin Server, to the relevant Channel Server, which then sends it out to every Gateway Server around the world that currently has a client subscribed to that channel. Each of those Gateway Servers then fans the message out over WebSocket to every one of its connected clients that's subscribed.

```text
You hit send
  → HTTP POST to Webapp
  → Webapp persists the message (durability first)
  → Webapp hands off to an Admin Server
  → Admin Server notifies the correct Channel Server
  → Channel Server broadcasts to every subscribed Gateway Server, globally
  → Each Gateway Server fans the message out over WebSocket
  → It appears on every connected teammate's screen
```

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Persist+First%2C+Then+Broadcast"
  alt="Diagram of the durable write-then-broadcast message path"
/>

A more detailed breakdown on [ScaleWithChintan](https://scalewithchintan.com/blog/slack-message-fanout-architecture) adds that this whole "one message, many recipients" pattern has a name in distributed systems: fan-out. Sending a single message to a channel with a thousand members means that one message needs to become potentially a thousand individual delivery events and Slack's architecture leans on tools like Apache Kafka as a durable, ordered queue in the middle of this pipeline, specifically so a sudden burst of messages doesn't overwhelm any single part of the system.

## The Clever Trick: How 16 Million Channels Fit on One Machine

This is genuinely one of the more elegant ideas in the whole system, and it's worth slowing down for.

Here's the problem: Slack has an enormous number of channels and "channel," according to Slack's own blog, is actually a broader internal concept than just the channels you see in the sidebar; it also covers things like a specific user, a huddle, or a file. You obviously can't fit every single one of these on one server, and you don't want every server to have to know about every other server's business either.

Slack's own blog explains the solution: every channel's ID gets hashed, and that hash maps it to one specific Channel Server. At peak times, a single host ends up responsible for around 16 million of these channels. Imagine a massive library, where instead of one librarian trying to remember where every single book is, each book's title is run through a simple formula that always points to exactly one specific shelf no coordination meetings required, no checking with other librarians, just a consistent rule everyone trusts.

The really clever part is what happens when a shelf breaks. Slack's blog describes a system called CHARM (Consistent Hash Ring Manager) that manages this hashing scheme and can replace an unhealthy Channel Server in under 20 seconds. Because a given team's channels are deliberately spread out across many different Channel Servers rather than clustered on just one, losing a single server only causes a brief, localized blip a small number of teams see slightly delayed messages for well under 20 seconds, rather than a large chunk of the platform going down at once.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Consistent+Hashing%3A+One+Shelf%2C+One+Rule"
  alt="Diagram of consistent hashing distributing channels across servers"
/>

## Handling the World's Time Zones

Usage isn't just "busy during the day, quiet at night" it's busier at different times depending on which part of the world you're looking at. The same blog post notes that on a typical workday, most users are online between 9am and 5pm local time, with visible peaks around 11am and 2pm and a small dip for lunch but because Slack operates globally, those peaks land at completely different points on a single global clock depending on the region.

Think of it like a relay race that never actually stops as one region's workday winds down, another's is just ramping up, so the system as a whole never really gets a quiet moment to rest. This is exactly why the architecture leans so heavily on horizontal scaling spreading load across many independent, replaceable machines rather than relying on any single, larger server that might have brief windows of low usage to recover in.

## Handling Duplicate Messages and Reconnections

A relatable, very human problem: your Wi-Fi flickers for half a second while you're typing, your client quietly retries sending the message just in case, and now there's a chance the same message could get sent twice.

The same [Talent500 breakdown](https://talent500.com/blog/slack-architecture-real-time-messaging/) explains Slack's fix: idempotency keys. Each message gets tagged with a unique identifier when it's created, and if the same message arrives twice because of a client retry, the server recognizes the duplicate key and quietly suppresses the second copy instead of showing it twice. The same piece notes this pattern extends further into the system's job-processing pipeline too, where Kafka handles durable, ordered message queuing acting as the system's permanent ledger while Redis handles fast, in-flight job data for the parts of the pipeline that need quick, temporary state rather than long-term durability.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Idempotency+Keys%3A+Same+Message%2C+Sent+Only+Once"
  alt="Diagram of duplicate message suppression using idempotency keys"
/>

## Presence: The Green Dot That's Harder Than It Looks

It seems like such a small feature a little green dot next to someone's name but it hides a genuinely interesting scaling decision. A write-up on [Medium](https://medium.com/@akashjha9041/real-time-messaging-1b860762524c) explains that Presence Servers track which users are online, and this is the clever part a Slack client doesn't actually get notified about every single person's online status across the whole platform. It only receives presence updates for the specific subset of users currently visible on that person's screen at any given moment.

That's a small but meaningful optimization. Broadcasting every user's online/offline status to every connected client, all the time, would be an enormous, mostly wasted amount of traffic. Only sending updates for the handful of people you can actually see on screen right now keeps the system honest about what information is actually worth the cost of sending.

## Multi-Region Resilience

An entire data center region goes down. What happens to everyone connected through it? The same Medium write-up notes that Gateway Servers, unlike the other core services, are deliberately deployed across multiple geographical regions, specifically so a client can connect to whichever one is nearest to it. When an entire region has problems, a draining mechanism seamlessly shifts affected users over to the nearest healthy region instead.

Think of it like a highway system with multiple bridges connecting two cities. If one bridge needs to close for repairs, traffic doesn't just stop it gets redirected to the next nearest bridge, and most drivers barely notice the detour.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Persistent+Connections%2C+Consistent+Hashing%2C+Global+Resilience"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing Slack's real-time architecture"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are exotic. WebSockets aren't new. Consistent hashing isn't new. Durable message queues aren't new. What makes Slack's architecture genuinely impressive is how deliberately each piece was separated out to do exactly one job well a server that only tracks presence, a server that only manages channel broadcast, a hashing scheme that quietly spreads 16 million channels across a fleet without anyone needing to coordinate so that the end result, according to Slack's own blog, is a system that delivers messages across the entire world in about 500 milliseconds, scaling roughly linearly as more customers join.

## What This Means Going Forward

As workspaces keep growing into organizations of hundreds of thousands of people, and as features like huddles, canvases, and AI-powered search get bolted onto the same real-time backbone, the fundamental challenge doesn't change: every new feature is, at some level, another kind of event that needs to reach the right subset of screens, instantly, without anyone waiting or losing a message along the way. The lesson underneath Slack's story is the same one running through every platform in this series: a product that feels instantaneous is almost always the visible tip of a system built, quite deliberately, to never make the user wait.


---

## How Vercel Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-vercel-works
Published: 2026-07-14T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Vercel's architecture the deployment pipeline, the global edge network, and the serverless and edge functions that let a git push turn into a live, fast website anywhere in the world.


You push code to GitHub. A minute later, a link shows up: `your-app.vercel.app`, already live, already fast, already working for anyone in the world who clicks it. No server to rent, no Nginx config to write, no "which region should I deploy to" decision to agonize over. It just works.

That simplicity is the whole product. And like most things that feel effortless on the surface, it's hiding a genuinely well-engineered system underneath. This article walks through what actually happens between your `git push` and that live URL in plain language, with relatable comparisons along the way. Most of this comes straight from Vercel's own engineering blog, so we get a lot of this one directly from the source.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=git+push+%E2%86%92+Live+URL"
  alt="Illustration of code being pushed and instantly becoming a live website"
/>

## The Two Halves of the Story

There are two completely different phases involved, and they happen at completely different times. There's the **build phase** turning your code into something servable, which happens once, right after you push. And there's the **request phase** serving that finished result to actual visitors, which happens constantly, every time anyone loads your site.

Vercel's own engineering blog, in a post titled [Behind the Scenes of Vercel's Infrastructure](https://vercel.com/blog/behind-the-scenes-of-vercels-infrastructure), walks through both halves in detail, and that's the structure we'll follow here too.

## Phase One: What Happens When You Push Code

Think of this like sending a manuscript to a print shop. You don't hand a reader your Word document the print shop first typesets it, prints it, binds it, and only then is it ready to go on a shelf. Your code goes through something similar before it's ready to be visited.

According to Vercel's own blog, a deployment can start from any of over 35 supported frameworks, triggered either through the Vercel CLI or by simply pushing to your connected git repository. A walkthrough on [Medium](https://medium.com/@mayank2803sharma/demystifying-vercel-how-it-works-and-how-to-create-vercel-763586070478) breaks this into simple steps: your project's files first get uploaded to durable cloud storage (conceptually similar to Amazon S3), and once that upload succeeds, a separate request kicks off the actual build.

A more recent guide from [FocusReactive](https://focusreactive.com/what-is-vercel/) describes what happens inside that build step: Vercel automatically detects your framework from your project's configuration, restores your dependencies from a cache if your lockfile hasn't changed (skipping a slow reinstall), runs your actual build command, and then this is the clever part inspects the output and classifies every single file into one of three buckets: a static asset to be served straight from a CDN, a Serverless Function, or an Edge Function.

```text
git push
  → Files uploaded to durable storage
  → Build container provisions resources, runs your build
  → Vercel inspects the output:
      - Static file? → goes to static storage / CDN
      - Server-rendered page or API route? → becomes a Serverless Function
      - Middleware or edge-runtime code? → becomes an Edge Function
  → Deployment metadata is saved
  → Deployment is now ready to serve on Vercel's network
```

Vercel's own blog confirms this last step directly: once resources are provisioned, a deployment record is created and its metadata is uploaded to static storage and that metadata is exactly what gets used later to route an incoming visitor to the correct resource, based on the path they requested.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Static%2C+Serverless%2C+or+Edge%3F"
  alt="Diagram showing build output classified into static, serverless, and edge buckets"
/>

## Phase Two: What Happens When Someone Actually Visits Your Site

This is the part that happens millions of times a day, and it needs to be fast every single time. Think of it like a really well-run reception desk at a huge building. It doesn't matter how big the building is the receptionist instantly knows exactly which floor, which room, and which department to send you to, without you waiting around while they figure it out.

Vercel's own blog walks through the request lifecycle starting from the very first step: a browser has to perform a DNS lookup before it can even reach the site, and for anything hosted on Vercel, that lookup always resolves to an anycast IP address that Vercel itself owns. Anycast is worth pausing on it means the exact same IP address is announced from many locations around the world simultaneously, and your request automatically gets routed to whichever one is physically closest to you, without you or your browser having to know anything about where that is.

Once the request lands at the right location, the same blog post explains the gateway's decision-making gets genuinely nuanced, depending on what was actually requested:

- **Static resources** (a static page, a font, an unoptimized image) the gateway just pulls it directly from static storage.
- **Vercel Functions** (a server-rendered page or API route) the gateway triggers a Serverless Function execution, specifically in the region that function was deployed to.
- **Edge Functions** the gateway formats the request to invoke execution right at the edge location itself, rather than a specific backend region.
- **Pages using Incremental Static Regeneration** the gateway first checks whether this exact page has already been rendered and cached before deciding whether to serve the cached version or regenerate it.

```text
Browser → DNS lookup → Vercel's anycast IP (nearest location)
  → Gateway inspects the request
      - Static file → serve from storage directly
      - Serverless Function → route to its deployed region, execute
      - Edge Function → execute right here, at this edge location
      - ISR page → check cache first, regenerate if needed
  → Response streamed back to the browser
```

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Anycast%3A+Same+Address%2C+Nearest+Door"
  alt="Diagram of anycast routing sending requests to the nearest edge location"
/>

## Serverless Functions vs. Edge Functions: Two Very Different Tools

This distinction trips a lot of developers up, so it's worth being plain about it. Both let you run backend code without managing a server but they're built for genuinely different jobs, and picking the wrong one for a task actually costs you real performance.

A guide on [Upstash's blog](https://upstash.com/blog/vercel-edge) explains the core difference simply: your code, when deployed as an Edge Function, effectively gets replicated across Vercel's edge locations, so when a user calls your endpoint, it executes at whichever location is physically closest to them cutting out the network latency that comes from the distance between a user and a single, centralized server.

An interview with Vercel's CTO, Malte Ubl, on [The New Stack](https://thenewstack.io/vercel-brings-serverless-functions-to-the-edge/), puts real numbers on why this matters: traditional serverless setups involve dozens to hundreds of milliseconds of cold-start time, while Edge Functions cut that down to just dozens of milliseconds largely because traditional serverless functions run inside a full microVM with its own startup process, while the edge runtime is a much lighter V8 JavaScript engine that can spin up far faster.

A Serverless Function is like calling a specialist at a specific office they might take a little longer to pick up, but they have access to the full toolkit and can handle heavy, complicated work. An Edge Function is like asking a quick question to whoever's standing nearest to you right now nearly instant, but best suited for fast, lightweight decisions rather than heavy lifting.

That's exactly the guidance in the same New Stack interview: Ubl notes that not every location is equally trusted or suited for every job running a cache somewhere is fine, but you don't necessarily want your core compute logic sitting just anywhere, which is part of why Edge Functions are intentionally kept to a smaller set of well-chosen global locations rather than absolutely everywhere.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Serverless%3A+The+Specialist%2C+Edge%3A+The+Nearest+Desk"
  alt="Diagram contrasting Serverless Functions and Edge Functions"
/>

### A relatable example: where should your code actually run?

- Checking if a user is logged in before showing a page → a great fit for an Edge Function. Fast, simple, needs to happen close to the user, before the page even loads.
- Processing a payment or querying a database → better suited to a Serverless Function, since it needs proximity to your actual data and the heavier tooling a full runtime provides.
- Serving your homepage's hero image → doesn't need either it's just a static file, served straight from the CDN.

A practical guide on [Techtic](https://www.techtic.com/blog/how-vercel-works-beginner-friendly-guide/) reinforces this same idea, noting that Edge compute isn't meant for heavy computation, and that authentication checks in particular should almost always run at the edge for maximum speed while also pointing out a common mistake: developers reaching for a fully server-rendered page when a much cheaper, faster static page would have done the job just as well.

## The Tricky Problem: Streaming Responses From a Serverless Function

Here's a genuinely interesting engineering problem, and a good example of Vercel solving a limitation in the tools they build on top of, rather than just accepting it.

Vercel's own engineering blog, in a post on [streaming from serverless functions](https://vercel.com/blog/streaming-for-serverless-node-js-and-edge-runtimes-with-vercel-functions), explains that their serverless architecture is largely powered by AWS Lambda and AWS Lambda, at least at the time that post was written, didn't natively support returning a response as a stream of data. That's a real problem for something like an AI chatbot response that needs to appear word by word, rather than all at once after a long wait.

The same post describes the workaround: when a request comes in for a Serverless Function with streaming enabled, it's first routed through an edge location, which forwards it to a dedicated invocation service. That service modifies the request to include a callback URL specifically for streaming, before forwarding it on to the actual Lambda function. Inside Lambda, custom bridge code written specifically by Vercel's engineering team uses that callback URL to open a connection back to the invocation service, streaming the response back in chunks as it's generated, rather than waiting for the entire thing to finish first.

```text
Client requests a streaming response
  → Edge location forwards request to Invocation Service
  → Invocation Service adds a callback URL, forwards to AWS Lambda
  → Lambda generates the response
  → Custom bridge code streams chunks back via the callback URL
  → Invocation Service relays each chunk back to the client immediately
  → Client renders content as it arrives, instead of waiting for it all
```

Imagine ordering a coffee, and instead of waiting silently until the entire drink is made, the barista hands you the espresso shot the second it's pulled, then the milk right after, then the foam last you start enjoying the coffee immediately instead of standing there watching the whole process with nothing to show for it. That's the actual user-facing benefit of this entire, fairly involved piece of bridge engineering.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Streaming%3A+Serving+Chunks%2C+Not+Waiting+for+the+Whole+Thing"
  alt="Diagram of the custom streaming bridge built on top of AWS Lambda"
/>

## Caching: Doing the Work Once, Serving It Many Times

A guide from [Fishtank](https://www.getfishtank.com/insights/what-is-vercel) describes Vercel's approach as multi-layered caching across the browser, the edge, and the origin server all working together to speed up load times without every single request having to redo the same work from scratch.
