The same guide notes this pairs with automatic image optimization (resizing and compressing files automatically) and Incremental Static Regeneration, which lets static content get refreshed in the background without requiring a full site rebuild every time something small changes.

It's the difference between a restaurant cooking a fresh batch of soup for every single customer, versus making a big pot once and serving bowls from it all day, only cooking a fresh batch when it actually starts running low. Static pages and cached responses mean most visitors are just getting a bowl from the pot that's already made fast, cheap, and consistent while ISR makes sure that pot gets quietly refreshed in the background instead of going stale.

## Security and Reliability, Built In by Default

The same [Fishtank guide](https://www.getfishtank.com/insights/what-is-vercel) notes that Vercel provides a built-in security layer as a standard part of the platform automatic HTTPS with free SSL certificates, DDoS protection described as mitigating billions of malicious requests weekly, and a customizable Web Application Firewall. The appeal here is less about any single feature and more about what it replaces: the same guide points out plainly that keeping infrastructure secure and scalable normally requires continuous monitoring, maintenance, and a dedicated team of experienced engineers work that Vercel is explicitly designed to absorb on your behalf, so an individual developer or small team doesn't have to become infrastructure security experts just to ship a website.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Push+Code%2C+Let+the+Platform+Worry"
  alt="Closing illustration representing Vercel handling infrastructure so developers don't have to"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are exotic inventions. A CDN is old technology. Serverless functions have existed for years. Even the AWS Lambda streaming workaround is, at its core, just clever request-forwarding. What makes Vercel's platform genuinely impressive isn't any single piece it's how invisibly all of them cooperate: a build step that automatically sorts your code into the right category, a global network that always finds the nearest door in for a visitor, and two different kinds of functions, each quietly picked for the job it's actually good at, so that a developer just writes code and pushes it, and the platform handles the hundred small decisions in between.

## What This Means Going Forward

As frameworks lean further into things like AI-generated responses, real-time collaboration, and increasingly personalized pages rendered per-visitor, the pressure on this kind of infrastructure only grows more requests need edge-level decisions, more responses need to stream instead of arriving all at once, and more of the "should this run centrally or right next to the user" decisions need to happen automatically rather than being left to a developer to configure by hand. The lesson underneath Vercel's story isn't really about serverless functions or CDNs specifically. It's the same one running through every platform in this series: the parts that feel effortless to the end user are almost always the parts an engineering team spent the most effort making invisible.


---

## How NotebookLM Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-notebooklm-works
Published: 2026-07-13T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into how NotebookLM turns your own documents into grounded answers, audio podcasts, and research reports, the retrieval pipeline, and the long-context Gemini model.


Upload a stack of PDFs to NotebookLM, ask it a question, and it answers using only what you gave it with little citation markers you can click to jump straight to the exact paragraph it pulled from. Ask it for an "Audio Overview" instead, and a few minutes later, two AI hosts are casually riffing about your document like they read it on the train this morning, complete with "oh, totally" and a well-placed pause.

Those two experiences feel like completely different products. Under the hood, they share the same foundation. This article walks through how that foundation is built the retrieval system that keeps answers grounded, the long-context model that makes it possible to reason across hundreds of pages at once, and the genuinely clever engineering behind making two AI voices sound like they're actually talking to each other. As with the rest of this series, this is a reconstruction from public interviews, engineering write-ups, and Google's own documentation, not NotebookLM's literal internal source code.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Your+Documents%2C+Grounded+Answers"
  alt="Illustration of documents feeding into a grounded AI assistant"
/>

## The Core Idea: Answer From the Notebook, Not From Memory

You ask about your own document, and it confidently blends what you gave it with things it half-remembers from training sometimes so smoothly you can't tell which parts came from where. NotebookLM's entire pitch is refusing to do that.

A guide from [DigitalOcean](https://www.digitalocean.com/resources/articles/what-is-notebooklm) describes NotebookLM as a source-grounded AI research assistant, built by Google and powered by Gemini, that uses retrieval-augmented generation to provide responses backed by citations. A write-up from [XsOne Consultants](https://xsoneconsultants.com/blog/notebooklm-audio-overview/) puts it plainly: when you upload documents, the system grounds its responses in that specific data, which meaningfully cuts down on the made-up-sounding answers you'd get from a more open-ended chatbot, because the AI is explicitly instructed to work only from the material you gave it.

Think of it like the difference between asking a friend who's read your report cover to cover, versus a friend who skimmed a similar report once last year and is now guessing. Both might sound equally confident. Only one is actually right.

```text
You upload: PDFs, Google Docs, web links, YouTube transcripts, audio files
  → Everything gets converted into a shared text representation
  → Text is chunked and indexed for retrieval
  → You ask a question
  → Relevant chunks are retrieved
  → Gemini generates an answer using only those chunks
  → Each claim gets a clickable citation back to the source
```

## Turning Everything Into the Same Shape

You can hand NotebookLM a PDF, a Google Slides deck, a YouTube link, or an audio recording, and it treats them all the same way. That's not free someone had to build a pipeline that flattens wildly different formats into one common shape before any AI reasoning can happen.

A detailed technical breakdown, shared as an internal-framework analysis on [Scribd](https://www.scribd.com/document/887551310/NotebookLM-Internal-Framework-Explained), describes this as a core preliminary step: converting every diverse input format into a unified textual representation. The same analysis notes that when you give it a YouTube URL, NotebookLM primarily imports the video's text transcript rather than analyzing the video frame by frame, and that audio files go through transcription first, turning spoken words into text that becomes the actual basis for any later analysis.

That same write-up flags an interesting, very real limitation this creates: for Google Docs and Slides specifically, this conversion tends to happen at upload time, which means edits you make to a live Google Doc afterward aren't automatically re-synced the notebook is working from more of a snapshot than a live connection, unless you manually refresh it. Building true always-fresh syncing for a constantly-changing collaborative document is a much harder, more event-driven engineering problem than processing a static PDF once.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Different+Formats%2C+One+Shared+Pipeline"
  alt="Diagram showing PDFs, docs, video, and audio converging into one text pipeline"
/>

## Why a Huge Context Window Changes the Whole Design

Most retrieval-augmented systems exist because of a hard constraint: language models can only "read" so much text at once, so you have to be clever about picking just the most relevant snippets and feeding them in. NotebookLM's underlying model changes that calculation somewhat.

A piece on [Turing Post](https://www.turingpost.com/p/fod69) explains that NotebookLM is powered by Google's long-context Gemini 1.5 Pro, a transformer model using a sparse Mixture-of-Experts design meaning it activates only the relevant parts of the model for a given input rather than the whole network every time, which keeps it efficient despite its size. The same piece notes this lets NotebookLM process up to 1,500 pages of material in a single pass.

A technical walkthrough on [DEV Community](https://dev.to/jubinsoni/architecting-the-future-of-research-a-technical-deep-dive-into-notebooklm-and-gemini-integration-m60) frames why that matters with a relatable comparison: a research project spanning 50 academic papers, roughly half a million words, would force a traditional model with a much smaller context window into complex retrieval gymnastics just to fit the material in piece by piece. A genuinely long context window means the model can instead hold a much bigger chunk of your material "in mind" at once which is exactly what lets NotebookLM compare methodology across five different papers, or spot a contradiction between two documents, in a single coherent answer rather than stitching together separate retrieval results.

This is a nice contrast to the Perplexity article earlier in this series: Perplexity leans hard on retrieval across the open web precisely because no context window could hold "the internet." NotebookLM's world is much smaller and more contained your own uploaded notebook so a large context window can do more of the heavy lifting directly.

## The Feature That Made Everyone Stop and Listen: Audio Overviews

This is the feature that turned NotebookLM from "a handy research tool" into something people were sharing clips of online. Two AI voices, chatting casually about your documents, sounding less like a script being read and more like an actual conversation.

An interview on [Latent Space](https://www.latent.space/p/notebooklm) with the feature's creators explains the core design decision plainly: a lot of earlier attempts at "AI podcasts" from other teams were really just glorified text-to-speech reading a script out loud. The NotebookLM team went a different direction, deliberately engineering the output to include small conversational touches brief interjections like "oh, really?" or "totally," along with natural pauses and hesitations the kind of small imperfections a real conversation has that a read-aloud script never would.

A breakdown on [Turing Post](https://www.turingpost.com/p/fod69) lays out the rough pipeline behind this: retrieval-augmented generation first pulls together the relevant material from your sources, prompt engineering structures how the two AI hosts should frame and discuss that material, a text-to-speech system generates the actual host voices, and a further step specifically injects those natural disfluencies the pauses and filler words into the generated dialogue to make it sound authentically unscripted rather than like a lecture with two narrators.

```text
Your sources
  → RAG retrieves and organizes key material
  → Prompted "script" generation: structures a two-host conversation
  → Disfluency injection: adds pauses, "umm," "right?", overlaps
  → Text-to-speech: generates two distinct voices
  → Output: a natural-sounding audio conversation
```

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Script+%2B+Disfluencies+%2B+Two+Voices"
  alt="Diagram of the audio overview generation pipeline"
/>

Imagine two versions of a friend explaining your notes back to you. One reads them out loud verbatim. The other actually discusses them, pauses to think, occasionally reacts. You'd remember the second one far better and that's exactly the gap this engineering work was built to close.

## From Answering Questions to Actively Researching

A more recent shift is worth calling out, because it changes what kind of system NotebookLM actually is. An analysis on [Medium](https://medium.com/@jimmisound/the-cognitive-engine-a-comprehensive-analysis-of-notebooklms-evolution-2023-2026-90b7a7c2df36) describes the introduction of a "Deep Research" capability as marking a shift from NotebookLM being purely a RAG tool retrieving answers from documents you already gave it toward something closer to an agentic researcher, capable of actively going out and finding new information on its own.

The same analysis describes the underlying workflow as a multi-step process: the system breaks a complex question into smaller sub-questions, runs searches across both the open web and your own private notebook in parallel, and then synthesizes the results explicitly checking for gaps in what it's found and generating new follow-up queries to fill them before giving you a final answer.

This mirrors a pattern that's come up more than once in this series Perplexity's Deep Research mode works in a very similar shape, decomposing a hard question before researching it in parallel rather than tackling it as one giant query. It's becoming a fairly standard blueprint for "AI actually does real research" features, not a NotebookLM-specific trick.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=From+Answering+Notes+to+Actively+Researching"
  alt="Diagram showing the shift from static RAG retrieval to an agentic research loop"
/>

## More Than Text: A Whole Studio of Outputs

Audio Overviews get most of the attention, but they're one output among several. The same [DigitalOcean guide](https://www.digitalocean.com/resources/articles/what-is-notebooklm) describes a "Studio" panel offering one-click generation of Audio and Video Overviews, mind maps, slide decks, infographics, data tables, and study aids like quizzes and flashcards all built from the same grounded source material sitting inside your notebook.

The interesting engineering point here isn't any single output format. It's that all of these are different presentations layered on top of the same core retrieval and reasoning pipeline. Once you've built the machinery to reliably pull the right information out of a pile of documents and reason over it correctly, generating a slide deck version versus a podcast version versus a quiz version becomes a matter of a different output template, not a fundamentally different AI system underneath.

## Where the Rough Edges Still Show

To keep this fair and balanced: NotebookLM's retrieval-grounded design meaningfully reduces made-up answers, but it doesn't erase the problem entirely. The same [XsOne Consultants write-up](https://xsoneconsultants.com/blog/notebooklm-audio-overview/) is upfront about this the AI hosts can still occasionally misread a nuance in the source text, or oversimplify a genuinely complex point for the sake of keeping the conversation flowing, and the piece recommends verifying critical details against the original source rather than trusting the generated summary blindly.

That's a healthy way to think about any AI system built on retrieval: grounding in your own sources makes answers far more trustworthy than a system guessing from memory, but "far more trustworthy" still isn't the same as "always correct."

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Grounded%2C+Not+Infallible"
  alt="Closing illustration representing careful, source-grounded AI output"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are new inventions on their own. Retrieval-augmented generation exists elsewhere. Text-to-speech exists elsewhere. Long-context models exist elsewhere. What made NotebookLM click with people wasn't any single breakthrough it was combining a genuinely large context window with disciplined source-grounding, and then caring enough about the small, unglamorous details of a natural-sounding conversation to make a document summary actually sound like two people talking, instead of a script being read aloud.

## What This Means Going Forward

As NotebookLM keeps shifting from a passive retrieval tool toward something that actively goes and researches on your behalf, the interesting engineering questions start to look a lot like the ones facing every AI research product covered in this series: how do you keep an increasingly autonomous system grounded and honest about what it doesn't know, how do you keep a growing pile of output formats all faithful to the same source material, and how do you make something that feels genuinely useful without quietly asking the user to just trust it. The podcast hosts might be the part everyone remembers but the real engineering story is the same one running underneath every product in this series: reliable retrieval, careful grounding, and a lot of unglamorous work making sure the flashy part on top actually deserves your trust.


---

## How Notion Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-notion-works
Published: 2026-07-12T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Notion's architecture the block-based data model at its core, how it scaled a single Postgres database to 200 billion blocks, and how real-time collaboration stays smooth across millions of users.


Open a Notion page and drag a to-do item under a heading. Turn a bullet point into a table. Nest a database inside a toggle inside another page. It all feels effortless, like the software is made of digital clay you can reshape however you want. That flexibility isn't a UI trick it's the direct result of one foundational decision made early on, and everything else in this article follows from it.

This piece walks through how Notion is actually built: the data model that makes all that flexibility possible, and the very real scaling battles that came with it once the product went from a few hundred users to over 100 million. Most of what follows is drawn directly from Notion's own engineering blog, so unlike some of the earlier articles in this series, we get to hear this one mostly straight from the source.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Everything+You+See+Is+a+Block"
  alt="Illustration of different Notion content types all sharing one structure"
/>

## The One Idea Everything Else Is Built On

Most productivity tools make you pick a lane. A document editor gives you paragraphs. A spreadsheet gives you cells. A task tracker gives you cards. If you want all three, you're juggling three different apps and copy-pasting between them.

Notion's own engineering blog explains the decision that avoided this: [everything you see in Notion is a block](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion) text, images, lists, a database row, even entire pages. They're all the same underlying object, just rendered differently depending on their type. A to-do item and an entire page are, structurally, close cousins. That single decision is why you can drag a paragraph into a table, or nest a whole database inside a bullet point, without the app needing a special case for every possible combination.

```json
{
  "object": "block",
  "id": "c02f...aea7",
  "type": "heading_2",
  "has_children": false
}
```

The same blog post notes this consistency is why features like "Turn into" work so smoothly changing a bullet point into a heading isn't a messy content conversion, it's just relabeling the block's `type` field while the rest of its structure stays exactly the same.

## Two Kinds of Pointers, for Two Very Different Jobs

Every block needs to know two different things: what it contains (so it can be rendered on screen), and who's allowed to see it (so permissions work correctly). You might assume these are the same relationship. They're not.

A breakdown on [HowWorks](https://howworks.ai/blog/how-notion-was-built) explains that Notion uses two separate relationships between blocks: a **content** pointer the ordered list of child block IDs used purely for rendering what's nested inside what and a separate **parent** pointer, used specifically for figuring out permissions. The same piece explains why these had to be split apart: historically, a single block could be referenced by more than one content array (the same block reused in two places), which makes "who owns this for permission purposes" genuinely ambiguous if you only had one relationship to work with. Walking upward through every possible content array to check permissions would also be painfully slow, especially on the client.

Think of it like a shared Google Photos album versus an actual physical photo album. A photo might appear inside three different shared albums (that's the "content" relationship where it visually shows up), but it still has exactly one place it was originally uploaded from, which is what actually determines who's allowed to delete it. Separating "where does this appear" from "who really owns this" is the same instinct at work.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Content+Pointer+vs+Parent+Pointer"
  alt="Diagram distinguishing rendering relationships from permission relationships"
/>

## What Happens the Instant You Type

You type a letter, and it just appears no spinner, no lag, even if your Wi-Fi briefly hiccups. Notion's own blog describes the flow behind that: your edit is applied **locally first, optimistically**, so the interface updates instantly without waiting for a server round-trip. That change is then sent to the server, which validates it against the "before" and "after" state for permissions and data correctness, and only then commits it to the database.

Once committed, the same [Notion engineering post](https://www.notion.com/blog/data-model-behind-notion) explains that a chain of background work kicks off: a version history snapshot gets scheduled, the block's text gets indexed for Quick Find search, and Notion's real-time updates service called MessageStore gets notified so the change can be pushed out to anyone else viewing that page.

```text
You type a letter
  → Applied instantly to your local view (optimistic)
  → Sent to server for validation
  → Committed to the database
  → Triggers: search indexing, version snapshot, real-time broadcast
  → Your teammate's screen updates, live
```

This "apply locally first, sync in the background" pattern is exactly why Notion keeps working when your internet briefly drops you're never actually waiting on the network to see your own typing show up.

## From One Database to Two Hundred Billion Blocks

This is where the story gets genuinely dramatic, and it's a great lesson in how "just add more servers" eventually stops being an option.

According to a technical write-up on [Relbis Labs](https://labs.relbis.com/blog/2024-04-18_notion_backend/), Notion started with a single PostgreSQL database, hosted on Amazon RDS, handling every request the product made. As usage grew, the team's answer was simple: make that one server bigger a strategy called vertical scaling. It's the database equivalent of upgrading to a bigger desk when your current one gets cluttered. That works for a while. It doesn't work forever.

A deep dive on [ByteByteGo](https://blog.bytebytego.com/p/storing-200-billion-entities-notions) describes what happened next: even the largest database instance Amazon offered eventually couldn't keep up, so the team had to split that single server into a cluster of many smaller ones a technique called sharding. Instead of one enormous desk, you now have many separate desks, each responsible for a specific slice of the work.

The critical decision was picking the right way to divide that work. A summary on [Lorenzo Palaia's blog](https://www.lorenzopalaia.com/blog/how-notion-scaled-to-handle-billions-of-blocks) explains that Notion chose **workspace ID** as the sharding key meaning every block belonging to the same team or user's workspace lives together on the same shard. That choice wasn't arbitrary: since users overwhelmingly work within their own workspace, keeping all of a workspace's blocks physically together avoids the slow, expensive "reach across multiple servers to answer one query" problem that comes with picking a bad sharding key.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Sharded+by+Workspace+ID"
  alt="Diagram of database sharding using workspace ID as the partition key"
/>

By 2023, according to [Relbis Labs](https://labs.relbis.com/blog/2024-04-18_notion_backend/), that cluster had grown to 96 database servers, each logically split into 5 shards and a more recent breakdown from [TechAhead](https://www.techaheadcorp.com/blog/tech-stack-powering-notion-block-based-editor/) puts the more current figure at 32 physical instances housing 480 logical shards, now handling more than 200 billion blocks with sub-second latency. That same piece notes Notion layers in read replicas to absorb roughly 90% of read traffic away from the primary databases, plus an in-memory Redis cache in front of everything for sub-millisecond lookups on the most frequently accessed blocks and PgBouncer, a connection pooler, sitting in between to stop the sharded fleet from getting overwhelmed by too many simultaneous connections.

### The migration itself had to happen without anyone noticing

Here's the genuinely hard part, and it's a very relatable engineering fear: how do you completely rebuild the foundation of a house while people are still living in it?

A write-up on [wildwildtech](https://wildwildtech.substack.com/p/how-notion-stores-the-data-and-scale) describes the technique: Notion used a "double write" strategy, writing every change to both the old, single database and the new, sharded one simultaneously, along with audit logs and a catch-up script to reconcile any drift between the two. The result, per the same piece, was a migration across a huge user base with only a brief window of downtime not the days-long outage you might expect from moving your entire data foundation.

## Why a Data Warehouse Alone Wasn't Enough

Here's a wrinkle that's easy to miss: most companies' data mostly gets **added to** over time new rows, new records. Notion's own engineering blog points out something different about their workload: [users update existing blocks](https://www.notion.com/blog/building-and-scaling-notions-data-lake) editing text, checking off to-dos, changing headings far more often than they create brand-new ones. About 90% of all block writes are updates, not inserts.

That matters because most data warehouses, including the Snowflake setup Notion originally relied on for analytics, are optimized for the opposite pattern: lots of inserts, not constant updates. The same blog post explains that ingesting Notion's genuinely unusual, update-heavy block data into that kind of warehouse became slower and more expensive as the company grew like trying to use a filing cabinet designed for adding new folders, when what you actually need is a system built for constantly rewriting the folders you already have.

The fix, according to the same post, was building a dedicated data lake offloading heavy data processing to Amazon S3 and using Apache Spark to handle the complex tree-traversal logic block data requires, only feeding fully cleaned, business-critical data into Snowflake afterward. This wasn't about replacing Snowflake entirely; it was about giving update-heavy workloads a home actually built for them.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Update-Heavy+Data+Needs+a+Different+Home"
  alt="Diagram contrasting insert-heavy and update-heavy data pipelines"
/>

## Where This Data Lake Pays Off: Notion AI

This is a nice example of infrastructure work quietly enabling a much flashier feature later. A recap of a Notion engineering talk on [Onehouse](https://www.onehouse.ai/blog/notions-journey-through-different-stages-of-data-scale) explains how the same data lake became the foundation for Notion's Q&A AI feature the one that lets you ask questions and get answers based on your team's entire Notion workspace.

To make that work, the team needed a vector database storing embeddings mathematical representations of chunks of text so the AI could look up the most relevant pieces of a workspace before answering a question. The same talk describes two update paths feeding that vector database: a large one-time batch job to bootstrap it for each workspace, and a continuous stream of incremental updates, broadcast through Kafka, whenever a block gets edited. Without years of prior work making update-heavy block data actually manageable at scale, a feature like this would have been a much harder problem to solve well.

## Putting It Together

None of these pieces are flashy on their own. A block is just a fancy name for "one small piece of content." Sharding is just "split your database into pieces." A data lake is just "store your data somewhere cheaper before cleaning it up." What makes Notion's engineering story compelling is the sequence: a genuinely elegant data model chosen at the very beginning, and then years of unglamorous, careful scaling work to make sure that same simple idea could still hold up under 200 billion blocks and 100 million users, without the person typing a to-do item ever noticing any of it.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=One+Block%2C+Two+Hundred+Billion+Times+Over"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing Notion's scaling story"
/>

## What This Means Going Forward

As AI features become a bigger part of how people use Notion asking questions across an entire workspace, summarizing pages, generating content the same block model and the same hard-won sharded, update-heavy infrastructure keep having to do double duty: serving instant edits to a person typing right now, while also feeding increasingly complex AI pipelines in the background. The lesson underneath it all isn't really about Postgres or Spark specifically. It's that a good, simple idea at the foundation one block, one shape, for everything can absorb an enormous amount of future complexity, as long as the infrastructure around it is willing to keep evolving.


---

## How Lovable Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-lovable-works
Published: 2026-07-11T00:00:00.000Z

How Lovable turns plain-English prompts into real, working full-stack apps.


You type one sentence "build me a leaderboard app where people can submit scores" and a minute later, there's a real, clickable app sitting behind a URL. No file setup, no "npm install," no blank VS Code window staring back at you. That moment feels like magic the first time you see it.

It isn't magic. It's a pretty clever pipeline of ordinary engineering ideas, stacked together carefully. This article walks through that pipeline in plain language, with everyday examples along the way. As with the earlier pieces in this series, this is a reconstruction based on public write-ups and Lovable's own blog, not their literal internal source code.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=One+Sentence+In%2C+One+App+Out"
  alt="Illustration of a text prompt turning into a running app"
/>

## From "GPT Engineer" to a $6.6 Billion Platform

Here's something worth knowing before we get into the pieces: Lovable didn't appear out of nowhere. According to [Muzli's guide to Lovable](https://muz.li/blog/lovable-for-designers-the-complete-guide-to-building-apps-with-ai-2026/), it started life as an open-source project called "GPT Engineer," which picked up more than 50,000 GitHub stars before relaunching commercially as Lovable in November 2024. The same guide reports it quickly passed 2.3 million users and $100 million in annual revenue, closing a $330 million funding round in December 2025 at a $6.6 billion valuation.

That growth curve matters, because it means Lovable isn't a weekend hack that got lucky it's a platform that had to keep working, and keep getting faster, while its user base exploded. That's a very different (and much harder) engineering problem than making a cool demo once.

## The Simple Version: What Actually Happens When You Type a Prompt

Think of it like ordering food at a restaurant through a really good waiter. You don't say "add two eggs, heat the pan to 350°F, whisk for 40 seconds." You just say "I'd like an omelet." The waiter (and the kitchen behind them) fills in every detail you didn't specify.

That's roughly the shape of what happens with Lovable. A review on [StartDesigns](https://www.startdesigns.com/blog/lovable-ai-app-builder-review/) describes the flow simply: you explain the application you want in plain language, and the system automatically generates interface components, backend routes, database structures, and authentication using real, modern code, not some proprietary drag-and-drop format.

```text
"Build me a leaderboard app"
  → AI plans the app structure (pages, data, logic)
  → Generates frontend code (React + Tailwind)
  → Wires up backend (Supabase: database, auth, storage)
  → Runs the code in a sandbox to check it actually works
  → Shows you a live, clickable preview
  → You describe a change → the loop repeats
```

## The Tech Stack Underneath: Nothing Exotic, Just Well-Chosen

A relatable comparison: a good chef doesn't invent new cookware for every dish. They pick reliable tools they trust, and combine them well. Lovable does the same thing with its technology choices.

According to a review on [StartDesigns](https://www.startdesigns.com/blog/lovable-ai-app-builder-review/), Lovable typically generates applications using React for the interface, Tailwind CSS for styling, and Supabase for backend services meaning authentication, the database, and file storage are all handled by an existing, battle-tested platform rather than something built from scratch. A deeper technical breakdown on the [ML6 engineering blog](https://www.ml6.eu/en/blog/the-anatomy-of-a-lovable-app-and-its-boundaries-in-enterprise-software) adds that this combination gives Lovable "fewer moving parts and fewer architectural choices" to generate correctly, which is exactly why it can produce a working app quickly there's less for the AI to get wrong when it's assembling proven building blocks instead of inventing new ones.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=React+%2B+Tailwind+%2B+Supabase"
  alt="Diagram of Lovable's core technology stack"
/>

## The Part Nobody Thinks About: Keeping Secrets Actually Secret

If an AI is writing your app's code automatically, how does it make sure your database password or payment API key doesn't end up sitting in the browser code where anyone can see it?

The same [ML6 breakdown](https://www.ml6.eu/en/blog/the-anatomy-of-a-lovable-app-and-its-boundaries-in-enterprise-software) explains this with a really useful mental picture: splitting every generated app into two "security zones." A blue zone code that runs in the user's browser, which only ever gets a limited, public key and is restricted further by database-level access rules. And a red zone sensitive operations and secrets, isolated inside server-side functions that the browser never directly touches.

Think of it like a hotel room key card. Your key card (the blue zone) lets you into your room and maybe the gym it's public-ish and limited on purpose. The hotel's master key system (the red zone) that can open every door in the building is never handed to a guest. Lovable's generated apps are built with that same instinct baked in from the start, not bolted on afterward.

```text
Browser (blue zone)
  → talks to Supabase using a public, limited key
  → protected by row-level security rules
  → can never see real secrets

Server (red zone)
  → holds API keys, payment secrets, sensitive logic
  → runs as Supabase Edge Functions
  → browser never touches this directly
```

## Why It Doesn't Just Write Code It Runs It

Frustration with older AI coding tools: they'd write you a code snippet, and you'd have to copy it, paste it, run it yourself, and only then discover it was broken. Lovable like similar tools such as Bolt and v0 skips that whole loop.

A detailed technical breakdown from [Beam](https://www.beam.cloud/blog/agentic-apps), a company that built their own Lovable-style clone to understand how these tools work, explains that apps like this need three core ingredients: a model client (think of your prompt as something like a function call to the AI), a sandboxed environment where generated code can actually run safely (small, isolated virtual machines or containers), and a way for the model to interact with that environment through a structured interface. The same breakdown makes an important point: the model doesn't just hand you code and walk away it runs the code in that sandbox, sees whether it worked, and can fix its own mistakes before showing you the result.

That last part is exactly like a student showing their work rather than just writing an answer on the board and hoping it's right. The system checks itself first.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Write+%E2%86%92+Run+%E2%86%92+Check+%E2%86%92+Fix"
  alt="Diagram of the generate-run-verify loop behind AI app builders"
/>

## The Real Secret Isn't a Smarter Model It's Better Guardrails

This is maybe the most useful, humbling insight from all the research: it's tempting to assume tools like Lovable work because the underlying AI model is unbelievably smart. The same [Beam engineering write-up](https://www.beam.cloud/blog/agentic-apps) pushes back on that directly, arguing that a big part of what makes these apps actually work in the real world isn't the model at all it's wrapping that model in guardrails: structured, typed prompts, automated tests, and retry logic when something goes wrong.

Imagine giving a very talented but slightly forgetful assistant a checklist for every task, instead of just trusting them to remember everything on their own. The checklist the structure, the tests, the "try again if this fails" logic is often what separates a tool that mostly works from one that reliably works. The same piece specifically calls out that good context (giving the model the right information about the existing app before asking for a change) matters even more than a cleverly worded prompt.

## Scaling the Platform Itself

It's easy to forget that Lovable is also just... a piece of software that needs to handle a lot of simultaneous users, the same as any other fast-growing product. According to [Lovable's own engineering blog](https://lovable.dev/blog), the team migrated their core platform from Python to Go specifically to boost concurrency meaning the system can juggle far more simultaneous requests without slowing down, since Go was built from the ground up for exactly that kind of parallel workload.

This is a nice parallel to the GitHub and Perplexity articles earlier in this series: the flashy AI-facing feature (turning a sentence into an app) only stays fast and reliable because of quieter, less glamorous engineering work happening underneath the kind of decision that never makes a demo video, but is exactly why the product doesn't fall over as millions of people use it at once.

## Editable, Not a Black Box

One more detail that matters a lot in practice: a review on [StartDesigns](https://www.startdesigns.com/blog/lovable-ai-app-builder-review/) points out that Lovable generates real application code rather than some proprietary format, meaning developers can keep working on the project outside the platform if they need to. Projects can also sync with GitHub, so echoing the GitHub article earlier in this series every change Lovable makes can flow through the same version-controlled, reviewable process a human engineer's code would.

That design choice matters because it avoids a trap a lot of no-code tools fall into: locking your project inside a platform you can never leave. Lovable's bet is that generated code should be a real starting point you can hand off to human engineers, not a dead end.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=A+Starting+Point%2C+Not+a+Dead+End"
  alt="Closing illustration representing exportable, editable generated code"
/>

## Where the Limits Show Up

To be fair and balanced here: this isn't a replacement for real engineering, and Lovable itself doesn't claim it is. A guide on [UI Bakery's blog](https://uibakery.io/blog/what-is-lovable-ai) puts it plainly a demo-ready application is not the same thing as a production-ready system, and generated code should be treated as a strong foundation rather than a finished product, especially once things like security review, load testing, and long-term maintainability enter the picture.

That's a healthy way to think about any AI-generated system: fantastic for getting from an idea to something clickable in minutes, but still worth a human engineer's eyes before it handles real users' real data at scale.

## Putting It Together

None of the individual pieces here are new inventions. Sandboxed code execution existed before Lovable. React, Tailwind, and Supabase all existed before Lovable. Typed prompts and automated retries are ideas borrowed straight from regular software testing practices. What makes Lovable work isn't one breakthrough it's combining all of these ordinary, well-understood tools around a language model, with enough guardrails that the AI's occasional mistakes get caught and fixed before you ever see them.

## What This Means Going Forward

As these tools keep maturing, the interesting engineering questions aren't really about making the underlying model smarter. They're about the same unglamorous questions every software team eventually faces: how do you keep secrets safe by default, how do you let a system check its own work, and how do you make sure speed today doesn't turn into a mess to clean up tomorrow. Lovable's story so far suggests the answer isn't more AI magic it's the same solid engineering fundamentals, just aimed at a new kind of user: someone who's never written a line of code, but has a really good idea.


---

## How GitHub Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-github-works
Published: 2026-07-10T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into GitHub's architecture  the Rails monolith at its core, the Spokes system that keeps every repository alive on three servers at once, and the scaling decisions behind a platform serving hundreds of millions of developers.


Every `git push` you've ever run has quietly triggered a small distributed-systems: three servers agreeing to accept your commit, a proxy making sure they all end up with identical bytes, and a background job deciding whether your pull request can still merge cleanly. You never see any of it. That invisibility is the whole point, and also the hardest part to build.

This article looks at how GitHub is put together from the giant Ruby on Rails application that still powers github.com, to the storage system that makes sure your repository survives a server catching fire. As with the earlier pieces in this series, this is a reconstruction from GitHub's own public engineering writing, not their literal internal source.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=One+Push%2C+Many+Moving+Parts"
  alt="Illustration of a git push triggering multiple backend systems"
/>

## The Monolith That Refused to Die

Here's something that surprises a lot of developers: github.com, the site handling code for what's now well over 100 million developers, is still at its core a single Ruby on Rails application. Not dozens of independent services, not a trendy microservices mesh from day one. One big app.

According to [GitHub's own engineering blog](https://github.blog/engineering/architecture-optimization/), that monolith has grown into nearly two million lines of code, with more than 1,000 engineers collaborating on it and roughly 20 deployments happening every single day.

Think of it like a house that started as a small cottage and, instead of being torn down and rebuilt every time the family grew, kept getting new rooms added on carefully, without ever asking everyone to move out during construction. That's the monolith story: not a failure to modernize, but a deliberate bet that a well-maintained single codebase, with the right internal boundaries, can outscale a lot of the complexity that comes with splitting everything into services too early.

That doesn't mean everything lives in the monolith forever. GitHub's engineering team has also written about breaking out containerized services to power specific microservices on github.com and various internal tools, growing that architecture around the monolith rather than instead of it, according to the same [GitHub architecture blog](https://github.blog/engineering/architecture-optimization/).

## What Actually Happens When You `git push`

You type `git push`, wait a second or two, see "Everything up-to-date" or a list of new objects, and move on with your day. Under the hood, a lot has to happen correctly in that second.

A detailed community write-up on [DEV Community](https://dev.to/pankajtanwarbanna/how-does-github-store-millions-of-repo-and-billions-of-files-3049) walks through the general shape of it: your request hits a load balancer, gets forwarded to a front-end server, passes through a reverse proxy into worker processes, and eventually reaches the Rails backend, which talks to MySQL and cache layers before touching the actual Git data.

```text
git push
  → Load Balancer
    → Front-end server (Nginx reverse proxy)
      → Application workers (Ruby on Rails)
        → Auth + permission checks (Metadata DB)
        → Git Service (validates objects, runs server-side hooks)
        → Durable write to repository storage
        → Reference (branch pointer) updated atomically
        → Event emitted → triggers CI/CD, search indexing, notifications
```

That last step an event being emitted after a successful push is what a system-design breakdown on [Medium](https://medium.com/@priyasrivastava18official/system-design-githubs-inner-game-the-architecture-that-swallows-the-world-s-code-and-doesn-t-c54fd53e4800) describes as the trigger for everything downstream: asynchronous indexer workers updating code search, and the CI/CD runner fleet picking up any workflows tied to that branch.

## Spokes: Why Your Repository Never Really Lives in One Place

This is, honestly, the most elegant piece of GitHub's infrastructure, and the one most developers never think about.

Imagine you're storing something precious say, the only copy of a family photo album. You wouldn't keep it in one drawer in one house. You'd want a copy somewhere else too, just in case. GitHub applies exactly that instinct to every single repository, at a scale of hundreds of millions of them.

According to a detailed engineering write-up by [Ajit Singh](https://singhajit.com/how-github-stores-and-serves-git-repositories/), GitHub stores every Git repository on three independent file servers, in a system called Spokes originally launched under the name DGit, for "Distributed Git." When you push, a proxy in front of the file servers streams your update to all three replicas, runs a three-phase commit, and only confirms success once at least two of the three replicas have applied the identical change.

GitHub's own [introductory blog post about DGit](https://github.blog/engineering/architecture-optimization/introducing-dgit/) explains the reasoning in a refreshingly simple way: Git itself is already a distributed system by design every clone contains the project's entire history so rather than bolt on a SAN or an exotic distributed filesystem, GitHub decided to lean into that property. Each file server just runs plain Git, on a normal Linux filesystem, on local disks. No abstraction layer between the code and the disk pretending to be clever.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Spokes%3A+Three+Copies%2C+Zero+Drama"
  alt="Diagram of three-way repository replication across file servers"
/>

### Why three copies, and why does it matter

A relatable way to think about the "majority rule": imagine three friends independently checking a math problem. If two of them get the same answer, you trust it. If all three disagree, something's wrong, and you don't move forward until it's sorted out.

A GitHub engineering post on [building resilience in Spokes](https://github.blog/engineering/infrastructure/building-resilience-in-spokes/) explains that no update to a repository is accepted unless a strict majority of replicas can apply the change and produce the same result. That single design decision means a repository can survive an entire server crashing which, at GitHub's scale, is not a hypothetical: the same post describes real incidents like a server going down due to a kernel deadlock or faulty memory chips, and Spokes routing around it automatically within a matter of failed operations.

```text
Push arrives → Proxy fans out to Replica A, B, C
  → Replica A: ACK
  → Replica B: ACK
  → Replica C: timeout (server unhealthy)
  → Majority (A + B) confirmed → push accepted
  → Spokes flags Replica C for automatic healing
```

### Going cross-continent without breaking

Here's where it gets genuinely tricky. Early versions of Spokes needed replicas to sit physically close together, because higher latency between them limits how many reference updates a repository can sustain per second. But close-together replicas are bad for disaster recovery you want a copy far away in case an entire region goes down.

GitHub's own post on [stretching Spokes across continents](https://github.blog/engineering/infrastructure/stretching-spokes/) lays out just how tight this budget can get: a single network round-trip across the continental US takes something like 60 to 80 milliseconds, and some repositories generate far more reference updates than you'd expect a single push to a popular branch can trigger test-merge and test-rebase computations for every open pull request targeting it, sometimes causing more than a hundred reference updates from one push. The same post describes solving this with an efficient "Spokes checksum" computed incrementally, as an XOR of hashes, so comparing whether two distant replicas actually match doesn't require re-scanning everything from scratch on every single update.

## Where Metadata Lives vs. Where Code Lives

It's worth separating two different problems GitHub has to solve, because they use very different tools:

- **Git object data** (your commits, trees, blobs) lives in Spokes, as plain Git repositories on local disk, exactly as described above.
- **Everything else** usernames, permissions, issue titles, pull request states, star counts lives in a more conventional relational database layer.

A write-up on [Ajit Singh's blog](https://singhajit.com/how-github-stores-and-serves-git-repositories/) notes that a separate MySQL/Vitess tier handles this repository metadata, while Git LFS (used for large binary files that don't belong in a Git history) stores its objects separately in object storage. This split matters because these two kinds of data have very different access patterns: Git objects are read constantly and rarely modified once written, while metadata like "is this branch protected" or "who has write access" changes far more often and needs strict consistency.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Code+in+Spokes%2C+Metadata+in+MySQL"
  alt="Diagram separating Git object storage from relational metadata storage"
/>

## Scaling the Database Layer Itself

A relational database sounds simple until you remember GitHub is handling requests from well over 100 million developers. It's the difference between one librarian managing a small town library, versus needing an entire coordinated system of librarians, branches, and a shared catalog to serve an entire country.

GitHub's own engineering blog describes ongoing work to [partition and shard their relational databases](https://github.blog/engineering/infrastructure/), a project set in motion specifically to meet growth and availability demands as the platform scaled. A Medium write-up on [GitHub's architecture](https://medium.com/@superdev7788/the-secret-sauce-behind-githubs-scale-379ed7a3a78b) adds color here too, describing high-read data being pushed into caching layers like Redis specifically to reduce load on the primary databases.

## CI/CD: Turning Every Push Into a Workflow Trigger

This is the part most developers interact with daily without thinking about the machinery behind it. Every time a workflow file exists in a repo, a push or pull request needs to reliably trigger builds, tests, and deployments potentially for an enormous number of repositories, all at once, all over the world.

GitHub's own engineering team has written about [using GitHub Actions internally](https://github.blog/engineering/), including scaling their CI experience by adopting larger, more powerful runners, and even using macOS and Apple Silicon runners specifically to speed up building and testing their own iOS app. The general pattern, as the system-design breakdown on [Medium](https://medium.com/@priyasrivastava18official/system-design-githubs-inner-game-the-architecture-that-swallows-the-world-s-code-and-doesn-t-c54fd53e4800) describes, is decoupling: CI/CD workflows are handled by a separate, independently scalable runner fleet, coordinated by an event dispatcher, so a sudden burst of builds across thousands of repositories doesn't compete for the same resources as someone simply browsing a repo's file tree.

## Observability: You Can't Fix What You Can't See

At this scale, "just check the logs" stops being a real debugging strategy there are too many logs, spread across too many services, generated by too many simultaneous users. A GitHub engineering post on [tackling platform problems](https://github.blog/engineering/infrastructure/how-github-engineers-tackle-platform-problems/) frames the split nicely: product engineers build the feature, but platform engineers build and maintain the tools that let anyone building on top of the system detect and resolve issues quickly closer to being the people who make sure the workshop has the right tools, rather than building the thing on the workbench themselves.

The same GitHub blog references ongoing work using tools like eBPF to detect problems in deployment tooling, and dedicated efforts around lifecycle management and defense-in-depth observability for critical systems, reflecting a broader pattern: reliability isn't a single feature you ship once, it's a continuous engineering discipline layered across the whole stack.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Observability%3A+Seeing+the+Invisible"
  alt="Illustration of monitoring dashboards tracking distributed system health"
/>

## Putting It Together

None of these pieces are impressive in isolation. A monolith is just a big app. A three-replica storage system is just "keep backups." A CI runner fleet is just "run scripts somewhere else." What makes GitHub's infrastructure genuinely hard to build is how these unglamorous pieces have to stay correct and fast at the same time, for hundreds of millions of people, across every timezone, every single day without the average developer ever noticing that any of it exists.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=A+Monolith%2C+Three+Copies%2C+Zero+Downtime"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing GitHub's core architecture"
/>

## What This Means Going Forward

As GitHub leans further into AI-assisted development coding agents, automated triage, and workflows that trigger far more read and write activity per human action than a person typing commands ever would the same old questions get harder, not easier. How do you keep a decade-old monolith flexible enough for new product surfaces? How do you keep Spokes fast when a single automated agent might generate far more reference updates than any human ever did? The infrastructure lessons here aren't really about Git, or Rails, or MySQL specifically. They're about a much older idea: the boring, disciplined parts of a system are usually what make the exciting parts possible.


---

## How Perplexity Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-perplexity-works
Published: 2026-07-09T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into Perplexity AI's architecture from its Vespa-powered search backbone to the Sonar model family, multi-model routing, and how it turns a raw question into a cited, real-time answer.


Google gives you ten blue links and lets you do the reading. ChatGPT gives you a confident answer, sometimes from memory, sometimes wrong, and rarely tells you where it got its information. Perplexity tried to sit in the middle: ask a question, get a written answer, and get a clickable list of sources you can actually verify.

That middle ground sounds simple. It isn't. Building it means solving a search problem and a language-model problem at the same time, and making sure neither one slows the other down. Here's how the pieces fit together.

Like the earlier articles in this series, this is a reconstruction based on public engineering write-ups and Perplexity's own blog, not their literal internal source code.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Search+%2B+Generate%2C+In+One+Breath"
  alt="Illustration of search and generation happening together"
/>

## The Core Idea: Retrieval-Augmented Generation

Imagine asking a friend a factual question. A friend who just guesses from memory might sound confident and still be wrong. A friend who says "hold on, let me check three articles first, then I'll answer you and show you exactly which lines I used" is slower, but you trust the answer a lot more.

Perplexity is built to be that second friend. Instead of relying purely on what a language model memorized during training, every query first triggers a live search, and the retrieved documents are fed into the model as grounding material before it writes a single word of the answer. This pattern is commonly called retrieval-augmented generation, or RAG, and according to a technical breakdown on [RankStudio](https://rankstudio.net/articles/en/perplexity-llm-tech-stack), Perplexity's pipeline collects and ranks relevant snippets, feeds them into the model with a system prompt, and has the model answer using only that grounded text with citations linked directly back to the source.

```python
# Simplified illustration of the RAG flow behind a Perplexity-style query
def answer_query(query):
    sub_queries = decompose(query)              # break question into parts
    candidates = search_index(sub_queries)        # hit the search backbone
    ranked_snippets = rank_and_filter(candidates)  # keep only what's relevant
    grounded_answer = llm_generate(
        query=query,
        context=ranked_snippets,
        instruction="Answer using only the provided snippets. Cite each claim."
    )
    return attach_citations(grounded_answer, ranked_snippets)
```

## The Search Backbone: Why Perplexity Didn't Build Its Own Index From Scratch

Think about it like opening a restaurant. You could grow your own wheat, mill your own flour, and raise your own cows or you could buy good ingredients from trusted suppliers and spend your actual energy on the recipe. Perplexity made the second choice for search infrastructure.

According to a detailed engineering breakdown on [ByteByteGo](https://blog.bytebytego.com/p/how-perplexity-built-an-ai-google), Perplexity built its indexing and retrieval layer on top of Vespa, an open-source engine that handles the distribution, partitioning, and ranking of content across large clusters automatically. The same piece notes that Perplexity's crawling infrastructure tracks over 200 billion unique URLs, backed by tens of thousands of CPUs and a storage system holding more than 400 petabytes in hot storage alone, and that the index processes tens of thousands of update requests every second to keep results fresh.

That last number matters more than it looks. A search engine that updates once a day is useless for a question like "what just happened in that court ruling this morning." The same ByteByteGo piece points out that Vespa's architecture co-locates data, indexes, and compute logic on the same node which avoids the kind of network bottlenecks that would otherwise cripple a system operating at this scale.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Vespa%3A+The+Search+Backbone"
  alt="Diagram of distributed search index architecture"
/>

By leaning on an existing, mature system for the "solved" problem of distributed search, the same ByteByteGo report observes that Perplexity's relatively lean engineering team has been able to focus its effort on the parts that actually differentiate the product: the RAG orchestration logic, the Sonar model family, and a custom inference engine.

## Sonar: Perplexity's Own Model, Built for Speed and Groundedness

You've asked a friend a question, and instead of a long philosophical essay, you just want a quick, correct, well-organized answer. That's the exact job Sonar is built for.

Perplexity's own [blog post introducing the latest Sonar](https://www.perplexity.ai/hub/blog/meet-new-sonar) explains that the model is built on top of Llama 3.3 70B and further trained specifically to improve two things that correlate strongly with user satisfaction: answer factuality and readability. The same post reports that, running on Cerebras's wafer-scale inference hardware, Sonar generates answers at roughly 1200 tokens per second described as nearly instant compared to typical model response times.

Speed like that isn't just a nice-to-have. If Sonar took five seconds to start answering a simple factual question, it would feel slower than just typing the question into a regular search bar defeating the entire point of the product. According to the same Perplexity blog post, internal A/B testing found Sonar closely matching or exceeding user satisfaction compared to larger frontier models like GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, despite being a smaller, purpose-built model.

## Not One Model a Whole Roster, Routed Intelligently

This is the part that surprises a lot of people: Perplexity isn't really "one AI." A breakdown from [DataNorth AI](https://datanorth.ai/blog/perplexity-ai-what-is-it-and-why-is-it-important) describes it more accurately as a multi-model orchestration layer, combining the in-house Sonar family with a rotating set of frontier models from providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and xAI, with Sonar serving as the default engine for quick searches on the free tier.

Think of it like a hospital reception desk. A simple question gets handled by a nurse in two minutes. A complex case gets escalated to a specialist. Perplexity's routing layer does something similar for questions. The same ByteByteGo report mentioned earlier explains that this flexibility is managed by an intelligent routing system, using small and efficient classifier models to first work out the user's intent and the complexity of the query, before deciding which underlying model should actually generate the answer.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=One+Router%2C+Many+Models"
  alt="Diagram showing query routing between multiple underlying models"
/>

This is the same instinct behind ChatGPT's model routing and Cursor's per-feature model choices, covered earlier in this series match the model to the job, don't use a sledgehammer on every nail.

## When a Simple Answer Isn't Enough: Deep Research

Some questions can't be answered by a single search-and-summarize pass. "Compare the last five years of a company's R&D spending against its competitors" needs multiple rounds of searching, cross-checking, and synthesis closer to how a human analyst would actually work.

A write-up on [TURION.AI](https://turion.ai/blog/perplexity-ai-deep-research-comet-infrastructure-2026/) describes how Perplexity's Deep Research mode handles this: the model first decomposes the original question into smaller sub-queries, each aimed at a specific angle of the topic, then issues those sub-queries against the search infrastructure in parallel, rather than one at a time. The same piece notes this whole process, which might take a human researcher hours, typically completes in a couple of minutes.

```python
# Simplified illustration of a Deep Research style loop
def deep_research(question):
    sub_questions = decompose_into_subquestions(question)
    results = parallel_search(sub_questions)          # fan out, don't wait in line
    evidence = rank_and_deduplicate(results)
    report = synthesize_report(question, evidence)     # long-context model pass
    return attach_full_citation_trail(report, evidence)
```

The "parallel" part is doing a lot of work here. Firing off ten sub-queries at once and waiting for the slowest one is a lot faster than researching each sub-question sequentially the same batching instinct that shows up in GPU inference scheduling, just applied to search calls instead of matrix multiplications.

## Two Front Doors, One Engine Underneath

A guide on [Suprmind](https://suprmind.ai/hub/perplexity/) describes Perplexity as running on a dual-surface architecture: the consumer answer engine at perplexity.ai serving people through web and mobile apps, and a separate developer-facing API exposing the Sonar family for programmatic use. Both surfaces, the same guide notes, share the same underlying retrieval-augmented-generation pipeline, differing mainly in interface, pricing, and available features.

This is a familiar pattern for anyone who's built a product with both a consumer app and a public API you don't want to maintain two separate brains. You want one well-tested core, wrapped in two different doors, so an improvement to the retrieval pipeline benefits the free mobile user and the paying API customer at the same time.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Two+Front+Doors%2C+One+Shared+Engine"
  alt="Diagram of consumer app and developer API sharing one backend"
/>

## Why "Real-Time" Is the Hard Part

You ask a chatbot about something that happened this morning, and it confidently tells you about the world as of its training cutoff sometimes months out of date. Perplexity's entire pitch depends on not doing that.

The same Suprmind guide notes that Sonar models are not purely parametric knowledge models sitting on memorized facts every query triggers a fresh search against Perplexity's own index of the public web, with retrieved documents chunked, filtered for relevance, and injected into the model's context before generation, with the company reporting roughly a 24 to 48 hour average freshness on retrieval. That's a very different engineering problem than training a static model once and serving it for months. It means the crawling and indexing pipeline is a permanent, always-on part of the product, not a one-time setup step.

## Putting It Together

None of these pieces work alone. A blazing-fast Sonar model is useless if the search index behind it is stale or slow. A perfectly fresh index is useless if the routing layer sends every question to an expensive model that takes ten seconds to respond. The real engineering achievement here isn't any single component it's the orchestration: a distributed search system, a family of purpose-built and third-party models, an intelligent router deciding who does the work, and a citation layer that ties every claim back to something a user can actually click and verify.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x630/1a1a2e/ffffff?text=Search%2C+Route%2C+Ground%2C+Cite"
  alt="Closing illustration summarizing the Perplexity pipeline"
/>

## What This Means Going Forward

As more products race to add "citations" and "web grounding" as checkbox features, the harder problem is what Perplexity has been building for years underneath: crawling infrastructure that stays fresh at a global scale, a routing system that's actually good at picking the right model for each question, and a research mode that can decompose a hard question the way a human analyst would. Answers are the easy part to copy. The infrastructure that keeps them fast, fresh, and honestly sourced is the part that's hard to fake.


---

## How Cursor Actually Works
URL: https://udaykumar-dhokia.github.io/insidethestack/articles/how-cursor-ide-works
Published: 2026-07-08T00:00:00.000Z

A deep dive into how Cursor IDE blends a familiar code editor with AI  from codebase indexing to multi-model routing to how it applies a diff without breaking your file.


If you've written code in the last year, you've probably either used Cursor or been annoyed by a friend who won't stop talking about it. It looks like VS Code, feels like VS Code, and honestly _is_ VS Code under the hood but the moment you hit `Tab` and watch it finish your thought three lines ahead of you, it's clear something very different is going on underneath.

This article is a behind-the-scenes look at how an editor like Cursor is actually built the pieces that make "AI that understands your whole codebase" feel less like a gimmick and more like a co-worker who's read the entire repo.

Like the ChatGPT deep dive, this is an engineering-lens reconstruction based on public talks, documentation, and general practice in this space not Cursor's literal internal source code.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=A+Familiar+Editor%2C+A+New+Brain"
  alt="Cursor editor interface illustration"
/>

## Why Fork VS Code At All?

Imagine you're building a car, and instead of designing wheels, seats, and a steering wheel from scratch, you start with a really good car that already exists and rebuild the engine. That's what Cursor did with VS Code.

VS Code is open source, has a mature extension ecosystem, and developers already have muscle memory for it keybindings, themes, settings sync, the works. Building on top of it means Cursor didn't have to win the "is this a good editor" argument. It only had to win the "is the AI good" argument. That's a much smaller, much sharper problem to solve.

The catch: every time Microsoft ships a VS Code update, Cursor has to merge those upstream changes into their fork without breaking their own AI layer. It's less "build once" and more "maintain a permanent, moving fork" a very unglamorous, very real engineering cost hiding behind a slick UI.

## The Four Main AI Surfaces

Cursor isn't one AI feature it's several, each solving a different problem:

1. **Tab (autocomplete)** predicts your next edit, sometimes several lines away from your cursor.
2. **Cmd+K (inline edit)** select code, describe a change, get a diff applied right there.
3. **Chat** a sidebar conversation with context about your project.
4. **Agent/Composer mode** give it a task, and it plans, edits multiple files, runs commands, and iterates.

Each of these has different latency requirements, different context needs, and honestly, different models running underneath. That distinction matters a lot for how the system is built.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Tab+%7C+Cmd%2BK+%7C+Chat+%7C+Agent"
  alt="Diagram of the four AI surfaces in Cursor"
/>

## The Hardest Problem: Knowing What Context Matters

Imagine asking a new hire to fix a bug in a 200,000-line codebase. If you just hand them the whole repo with no guidance, they'll drown. If you only hand them the one file with the bug, they might miss that the real issue is in a config three folders away.

This is exactly the problem Cursor has to solve on every single keystroke or chat message: **what's actually relevant right now?**

### Codebase indexing

When you open a project, Cursor builds an index chunking your files and turning them into embeddings (numerical representations of meaning, not just text). This lets it later ask "what code in this repo is semantically related to this question?" instead of doing a dumb keyword search.

```python
# Simplified illustration of what indexing roughly does
def index_codebase(files):
    chunks = []
    for file in files:
        for chunk in split_into_meaningful_chunks(file):
            embedding = embed(chunk.text)
            chunks.append({
                "file": file.path,
                "text": chunk.text,
                "embedding": embedding,
                "start_line": chunk.start_line,
            })
    return build_vector_index(chunks)
```

### Retrieval at request time

When you ask "why is this API call failing," Cursor doesn't send your entire codebase to the model (that would be slow, expensive, and blow past context limits). Instead, it retrieves the handful of chunks most relevant to your question and stitches them together with:

- the currently open file
- your recent edit history
- terminal output, if relevant
- any files you've explicitly `@`-mentioned

```python
def build_context(query, open_file, recent_edits, index):
    relevant_chunks = index.search(query, top_k=15)
    return {
        "query": query,
        "open_file": open_file,
        "recent_edits": recent_edits[-10:],
        "retrieved_context": relevant_chunks,
    }
```

This is the same "retrieval augmented generation" idea used in a lot of AI products, just tuned specifically for source code where structure (imports, function calls, class hierarchies) matters as much as raw text similarity.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Retrieval%3A+Finding+the+Needle+in+the+Repo"
  alt="Diagram of context retrieval from a large codebase"
/>

## Why Tab Feels So Fast

If autocomplete took 3 seconds to show up, you'd have already typed the code yourself out of impatience. Tab has to be _fast_ under a few hundred milliseconds ideally which rules out routing every keystroke to a giant, slow model.

So Tab typically runs on a smaller, purpose-built model, fine-tuned specifically on the "predict the next edit" task rather than general conversation. It's also predicting something narrower than Chat is: not "write me a function," but "given what you were just doing, where are you probably about to type next."

That's why Tab sometimes jumps to a completely different line than your cursor it's not just completing text, it's guessing your _next intended edit location_, based on patterns like "you renamed this variable here, you probably need to rename it in these three other places too."

## Cmd+K: Editing Without Breaking Everything

Say you select a function and ask Cmd+K to "add error handling here." The model doesn't just delete your code and write new code from scratch that would be a great way to accidentally erase a comment or a subtle edge case you handled six months ago and forgot about.

Instead, the system typically:

1. Sends the selected code plus surrounding context to the model.
2. Gets back a proposed change, often as a diff rather than a full rewrite.
3. Applies that diff with a separate, smaller "apply model" that's specifically good at merging a proposed change into existing code precisely matching whitespace, preserving unrelated lines, resolving small ambiguities.
4. Shows you the diff so you can accept, reject, or edit further.

That "apply model" step is easy to overlook but it's doing real work. A large, powerful model is great at _reasoning_ about what should change; it's not always precise about _exactly_ which characters to touch in a big file. Splitting "figure out the change" from "apply the change precisely" is a pattern that shows up a lot in AI coding tools.

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Propose+the+Diff+%E2%86%92+Apply+the+Diff"
  alt="Diagram showing diff proposal and precise diff application as separate steps"
/>

## Agent Mode: When the AI Drives for a While

Agent (or Composer) mode is the biggest leap you describe a task like "add pagination to this API endpoint and update the tests," and it:

- reads relevant files
- plans a sequence of edits across multiple files
- runs commands (like tests or a build) to check its own work
- iterates if something fails
- reports back what it did

Think of it like handing a task to a junior engineer and getting a checkpoint at each meaningful step rather than reviewing every keystroke. That "checkpoint" idea is important architecturally: agent mode isn't one giant model call, it's a loop plan, act, observe the result, decide the next action and the system needs to track enough state to let you undo or roll back if the agent goes down the wrong path.

```python
# Simplified agent loop
def run_agent(task, codebase):
    plan = model.plan(task, codebase.summary())
    for step in plan.steps:
        result = execute(step)  # edit a file, run a command, etc.
        if result.failed:
            step = model.replan(step, result.error)
            result = execute(step)
        checkpoint(step, result)
    return summarize_changes()
```

This is also where things get expensive fast multiple model calls per task, sometimes involving large models for planning and smaller ones for individual edits. Routing between model sizes here matters just as much as it does for a product like ChatGPT.

## The "Shadow Workspace" Idea

One neat, less obvious problem: if the agent wants to run your code or a linter to check its own work, it shouldn't do that in the actual file you're actively editing you might have unsaved changes, or you might be mid-thought on something unrelated.

So a common approach is to have the AI work in a hidden, parallel copy of your workspace sometimes called a "shadow workspace" where it can make edits, run checks, even execute code, without ever touching your visible editor state until it's confident the change is good. Only then does it surface the diff to you.

It's a small detail, but it's the difference between an AI assistant that feels safe to hand tasks to, and one that makes you nervous every time it starts "helping."

<img
  src="https://placehold.co/1200x500/16213e/e5e5e5?text=Shadow+Workspace%3A+Test+Before+You+Touch"
  alt="Diagram of a hidden parallel workspace used for safe testing"
/>

## Privacy and the Local vs. Cloud Question

A very relatable concern: nobody wants their proprietary codebase silently uploaded to train someone else's model. This shapes real architectural decisions:

- Embeddings for indexing are often computed in a way that avoids sending raw code to third parties unnecessarily.
- Enterprise setups often support private/self-hosted indexing or opting out of any data retention for training.
- There's a constant trade-off between "send more context to the model for a better answer" and "send less to respect privacy and reduce latency/cost."

This isn't just a policy checkbox it directly affects how much context the retrieval system is allowed to gather and send, which in turn affects answer quality. Privacy constraints are an architectural input, not an afterthought bolted on at the end.

## Multiple Models, One Seamless Feel
